title.zh: 卷六十七 黨錮列傳
title.raw: 卷六十七 黨錮列傳 Volume 67: Biographies of Partisan Prohibitions

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「性相近也，習相遠也。

translations.literal: Confucius said, "By nature men are near to one another; by practice they grow far apart."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Human natures start much alike; it is habit that sets them wide apart."

sentences.zh: 」言嗜惡之本同，而遷染之塗異也。

translations.literal: " It means that the root of likes and dislikes is the same, but the paths by which one is shifted and dyed differ.
translations.idiomatic: That is to say, what we love and hate begins from a common ground, yet the influences that reshape us diverge.

sentences.zh: [一]夫刻意則行不肆，牽物則其志流。

translations.literal: [1] If one forces and carves the mind, conduct will not run wild; if one is dragged along by things, the will drifts away.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When the mind is rigidly disciplined, behavior stays within bounds; when outer things pull you about, your purpose loses its anchor.

sentences.zh: [二]是以聖人導人理性，裁抑宕佚，慎其所與，節其所偏，雖情品萬區，質文異數，至於陶物振俗，其道一也。

translations.literal: [2] Therefore the sage guides people to order nature and principle, curbs excess and dissipation, is careful whom he associates with, and moderates partiality; though dispositions fall into myriad types and substance and ornament follow different measures, when it comes to molding things and stirring custom, the way is one.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Hence the sage teaches people to align character with reason, reins in recklessness and indulgence, chooses companions with care, and checks one-sided excess. Dispositions may differ in countless ways, and plainness or polish may not match the same scale, yet in shaping conduct and renewing the age, the underlying path is single.

sentences.zh: [三]叔末澆訛，王道陵缺，[四]而猶假仁以效己，憑義以濟功。

translations.literal: [3] In the late age, customs grew shallow and false, the kingly way crumbled and fell short, [4] yet men still borrowed benevolence to polish themselves and relied on righteousness to accomplish their aims.
translations.idiomatic: [3] In that decadent tail-end of the age, morals thinned and twisted and the royal road gave way, [4] yet rulers still wore humanity as a mask for self-display and invoked duty as a lever for ambition.

sentences.zh: 舉中於理，則強梁簡氣；

translations.literal: When action hits the mark of principle, then the overbearing shed their arrogance;
translations.idiomatic: When policy accords with right principle, the violent and proud are humbled;

sentences.zh: 片言違正，則□台解情。

translations.literal: when a scrap of speech strays from rectitude, then the base man (tai) twists the purport of the matter.
translations.idiomatic: when a single skewed phrase departs from the straight path, even a low-born go-between can bend the truth of the case to suit himself.

sentences.zh: 蓋前哲之遺塵，有足求者。

translations.literal: For in the dust-trails left by former wise men there is enough worth pursuing.
translations.idiomatic: The footprints the ancients left behind still repay our study.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]嗜猶好也。

translations.literal: [5] Comment [1]: shi (嗜) means "to like."
translations.idiomatic: [5] Note [1]: here shi means "to be fond of" or "to crave."

sentences.zh: 惡音烏故反。

translations.literal: For wu (惡, "hate"), the reading is the wu-gu fan cut (烏故反).
translations.idiomatic: The character wu (惡) in this sense is read like wu in the fanqie pair wu-gu.

sentences.zh: 言人好惡，各有本性，遷染者，由其所習。

translations.literal: It says that in people's likes and dislikes each has his inborn nature; shifting and dyeing come from what they practice.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that attraction and aversion rest on innate disposition, while what shifts and stains character is whatever habit has ingrained.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰：「唯人生厚，因物有遷。

translations.literal: The Documents says, "Only man is born thick [in feeling]; because of things there is shifting.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Documents says, "Man alone is richly endowed at birth, yet circumstance moves him this way and that."

sentences.zh: 」墨子曰：「墨子見染絲者，泣而歎曰：『染於蒼則蒼，染於黃則黃，故染不可不慎也。

translations.literal: " Mozi said, "When Mozi saw the dyer of silk, he wept and sighed, saying, 'Dyed in dark blue it becomes dark blue; dyed in yellow it becomes yellow; therefore dyeing must not be treated lightly.
translations.idiomatic: " Mozi records how he watched a silk dyer weep and sigh: 'Soak it in indigo and it turns indigo; soak it in yellow and it turns yellow—so the vat you choose is never a small matter.'"

sentences.zh: 非獨染絲然也，國亦有染。

translations.literal: It is not only dyeing silk that is so; states also have dyeing.
translations.idiomatic: Silk is not the only thing that takes a dye; whole kingdoms do as well.'

sentences.zh: 湯染於伊尹，故王天下；

translations.literal: Tang was dyed by Yi Yin, therefore he became king over the realm;
translations.idiomatic: Tang absorbed the influence of Yi Yin and so came to rule the world;

sentences.zh: 殷紂染於惡來，故國殘身死，為天下僇。

translations.literal: Yin Zhou was dyed by Wu Lai, therefore the state was ruined, he died in person, and became the laughingstock of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: the last king of Shang took his color from Wu Lai, and so his realm was torn apart, he perished in disgrace, and posterity holds him up as a warning.'

sentences.zh: 』」注[二]刻意，刻削其意不得自恣也。

translations.literal: '" Comment [2]: ke yi (刻意) means carving and cutting the mind so it cannot indulge itself.
translations.idiomatic: '" Note [2]: "forcing the mind" means chiseling intent until it cannot run loose."

sentences.zh: 莊子曰：「刻意尚行，離時異俗。

translations.literal: Zhuangzi said, "Forcing the mind and prizing conduct, departing from the age and differing from custom.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi says, "Those who mortify the mind and make a cult of conduct cut themselves off from their own times and stand apart from common ways."

sentences.zh: 」行音下孟反。

translations.literal: " For xing (行), the reading is the xia-meng fan cut (下孟反).
translations.idiomatic: " The word xing (行) here is read with the xia-meng fanqie gloss."

sentences.zh: 肆猶族縱也。

translations.literal: The glossed word means reckless indulgence like a clan running wild.
translations.idiomatic: Here it signifies wanton excess, the kind that lets a whole faction run unchecked.

sentences.zh: 牽物謂為物所牽制，則其志流宕忘反也。

translations.literal: "Being dragged by things" means being controlled by things, so that the will drifts dissipated and forgets to return.
translations.idiomatic: "Pulled about by externals" means letting things steer you until your purpose wanders off and never finds its way back.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰：「非拘緊牽連於物，而不與推移也。」

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says, "It is not being cramped and tied and linked to things, yet not shifting with their changes."
translations.idiomatic: The Huainanzi puts it differently: true freedom is not clinging so tight to things that you cannot move with the times.

sentences.zh: 注[三]陶謂陶治以成之。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: tao means to mold and work so as to complete.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: tao here means shaping and firing, as a potter completes the ware.

sentences.zh: 管子曰：「夫法之制人，猶陶之於埴，冶之於金也。」

translations.literal: Guanzi says, "As for law's governing people, it is like the potter with clay, the smelter with metal."
translations.idiomatic: Guanzi says that law shapes a people the way a potter shapes clay or a founder tempers metal.

sentences.zh: 埴音植。

translations.literal: For zhi (埴), the reading is like zhi (植).
translations.idiomatic: The character zhi (埴, "potter's clay") is read like zhi (植).

sentences.zh: 注[四]叔末猶季末也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: shu mo is like ji mo, the end of a season.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: shu mo means the closing phase of an era, like the last month of a season.

sentences.zh: 謂當春秋之時。

translations.literal: It refers to the time of the Spring and Autumn period.
translations.idiomatic: That is, the late Spring and Autumn age.

sentences.zh: 注[五]褫猶奪也，音直紙反。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: chi (褫) means to strip away; the reading is the zhi-zhi fan cut (直紙反).
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: chi (褫) means to wrest away; pronounce it with the zhi-zhi fanqie.

sentences.zh: □台，賤人也。

translations.literal: Tai is a low-born person.
translations.idiomatic: The word tai denotes a person of mean station; the manuscript is damaged at the preceding graph.

sentences.zh: 齊侯伐楚，楚子使與師言曰：「君處北海，寡人南海，唯是風馬牛不相及也，不虞君之涉吾地也。

translations.literal: The marquis of Qi attacked Chu; the viscount of Chu sent someone to speak to the army, saying, "You dwell by the northern sea, I by the southern sea—only thus wind, horses, and cattle would not meet; I did not expect you to cross into my territory.
translations.idiomatic: When the marquis of Qi marched against Chu, the king of Chu sent a spokesman to the invaders: "You hold the north, we the south; even the winds of our pastures would never have brought our herds together. We never thought you would set foot on our soil."

sentences.zh: 何故？

translations.literal: What is the reason?
translations.idiomatic: What brings you here?

sentences.zh: 」管仲對曰：「爾貢苞茅不入，王祭不供，無以縮酒，寡人是征。

translations.literal: " Guan Zhong replied, "Your tribute of bao mao grass has not come in; the king's sacrifices are not supplied; there is nothing with which to strain the wine—I therefore demand an accounting.
translations.idiomatic: " Guan Zhong answered, "You have failed to send the bundled reeds owed as tribute, so the royal house cannot prepare its libations. That is what we have come to call you to account for.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「貢之不入，寡君之罪也。

translations.literal: " He answered, "If the tribute has not come in, it is my lord's fault.
translations.idiomatic: " The envoy replied, "If the tribute never arrived, the fault lies with our ruler.

sentences.zh: 」遂使屈完與齊盟於召陵。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he sent Qu Wan to covenant with Qi at Shaoling.
translations.idiomatic: " Qu Wan was then sent to conclude a pact with Qi at Shaoling."

sentences.zh: 此強梁褫氣也。

translations.literal: This is the overbearing having their arrogance stripped away.
translations.idiomatic: That episode shows how the violent are made to swallow their pride.

sentences.zh: 又晉呂甥、卻苪將焚公宮而殺晉侯，寺人披請見，公使讓之，且辭曰：「汝為惠公來求殺余，命汝三宿，汝中宿而至。

translations.literal: Again, Jin's Lü Sheng and Xi Rui were about to burn the ducal palace and kill the marquis of Jin; the eunuch Pi asked an audience; the duke sent to reproach him, and moreover declined, saying, "When you came for Duke Hui seeking to kill me, I ordered you to wait three nights, yet you arrived in the middle night.
translations.idiomatic: Again, when Lü Sheng and Xi Rui of Jin plotted to burn the palace and murder their lord, the eunuch Pi asked to see Duke Wen. The duke sent word of rebuke: "When you served Duke Hui and came hunting my life, I told you to wait three days, yet you showed up on the second night."

sentences.zh: 雖君有命，何其速也？

translations.literal: Though the lord had given orders, how could you be so swift?
translations.idiomatic: Even under orders, why were you in such haste?

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「臣謂君之入也，其知之矣。

translations.literal: " He replied, "I supposed that when my lord entered, he already understood.
translations.idiomatic: " Pi answered, "I assumed that by the time you returned, you would already have understood.

sentences.zh: 若猶未也，又將及難。

translations.literal: If you still have not, then you will again meet disaster.
translations.idiomatic: If you still do not, fresh calamity will overtake you.

sentences.zh: 君命無二，古之制也。

translations.literal: A lord's command admits no twofoldness—this is the regulation of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler's order brooks no divided loyalty—that was the ancient rule.

sentences.zh: 除君之惡，唯力是視，蒲人狄人，余何有焉。

translations.literal: Removing the lord's evil—only strength is looked to; whether men of Pu or of Di, what have I to do with it?
translations.idiomatic: To purge evil from one's lord, one brings whatever force one has; whether you were the man of Pu or the man of Di meant nothing to me.

sentences.zh: 今君即位，其無蒲、狄乎？

translations.literal: Now that my lord has taken the throne, will there not be Pu and Di?
translations.idiomatic: Now that you sit on the throne, must I still treat you as the exile of Pu or Di?

sentences.zh: 」此為□台解情也。

translations.literal: " This is the base man (tai) twisting the purport of the matter.
translations.idiomatic: " That is how a petty broker can twist the truth and still carry the day."

sentences.zh: 並見左傳。

translations.literal: Both are seen in the Zuo Commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Both anecdotes appear in the Zuo Commentary.

sentences.zh: 霸德既衰，狙詐萌起。

translations.literal: Once hegemonic virtue had declined, monkey cunning began to rise.
translations.idiomatic: When the moral force of the hegemons waned, sly trickery sprouted everywhere.

sentences.zh: [一]強者以決勝為雄，弱者以詐劣受屈。

translations.literal: [1] The strong took deciding victory as heroism; the weak, by fraud and inferiority, submitted in humiliation.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The powerful counted triumph everything; the weak survived only by guile, and paid for it in humiliation.

sentences.zh: 至有畫半策而綰萬金，開一說而錫琛瑞。

translations.literal: There reached the point of sketching half a stratagem and tying up ten thousand in gold, opening one persuasion and being granted precious jades.
translations.idiomatic: Men could sketch half a scheme and walk away weighed down with gold, or float a single persuasion and receive gems from a ruler's hand.

sentences.zh: [二]或起徒步而仕執珪，解草衣以升卿相。

translations.literal: [2] Some rose from walking on foot to serving holding a jade tablet; doffing rush-cloth garments they ascended to minister and chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Some marched straight from the road to the court with jade baton in hand; others shed their peasant cloaks and stepped straight into the ranks of chief ministers.

sentences.zh: [三]士之飾巧馳辯，以要能釣利者，不期而景從矣。

translations.literal: [3] As for gentlemen adorning cleverness and racing in disputation, in order to seek favor and hook profit—without awaiting a summons they followed like shadows.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Scholars who polished clever words and raced through debate to win patronage and angle for profit found a host of followers gathering like shadows without being called.

sentences.zh: [四]自是愛尚相奪，與時回變，其風不可留，其敝不能反。

translations.literal: [4] From this time loves and admirations wrested one another, turned and changed with the times; its wind could not be stayed, its decay could not be reversed.
translations.idiomatic: [4] After that, rival schools of fashion tore at one another and shifted with every season; the momentum could not be halted, and the rot could not be rolled back.

sentences.zh: 注[一]霸德衰謂六國時也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the decline of hegemonic virtue refers to the time of the Six States.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the waning of hegemonic virtue means the era of the Warring States.

sentences.zh: 狙音七余反。

translations.literal: For ju (狙), the reading is the qi-yu fan cut (七余反).
translations.idiomatic: Ju (狙) is read with the qi-yu fanqie.

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「狙，獮猴也。

translations.literal: The Guangya says, "Ju (狙) is the macaque.
translations.idiomatic: The Guangya defines ju as the macaque—

sentences.zh: 」以其多詐，故比之也。

translations.literal: " Because it is much given to deceit, therefore it is compared to them.
translations.idiomatic: "—and because the creature is full of guile, strategists are compared to it."

sentences.zh: 注[二]蘇秦說趙王，賜白壁百雙，黃金萬鎰。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Su Qin persuaded the king of Zhao and was granted a hundred pairs of white jade disks and ten thousand yi of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Su Qin talked his way into the favor of the king of Zhao and received a hundred pairs of white jades and ten thousand yi of gold.

sentences.zh: 虞卿一見趙王，賜白璧一雙，黃金百鎰。

translations.literal: Yu Qing, on one audience with the king of Zhao, was granted one pair of white jade disks and a hundred yi of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qing won a single audience with the same king and walked out with a pair of white disks and a hundred yi of gold.

sentences.zh: 見史記及戰國策。

translations.literal: See the Records of the Historian and the Intrigues of the Warring States.
translations.idiomatic: Both stories are recorded in the Shiji and the Zhanguo ce.

sentences.zh: 注[三]史記曰，楚惠王言「莊舄，越之鄙細人也，今仕楚執珪，貴富矣」。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: the Records of the Historian says that King Hui of Chu stated, "Zhuang Xi was a mean petty man of Yue; now he serves Chu holding a jade tablet—noble and rich."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: the Shiji quotes King Hui of Chu remarking that Zhuang Xi had been a nobody from the Yue backwoods yet now served Chu with jade baton in hand, rich and honored.

sentences.zh: 解草衣謂范睢、蔡澤之類。

translations.literal: "Doffing rush-cloth garments" refers to the likes of Fan Ju and Cai Ze.
translations.idiomatic: "Shedding the rush cloak" describes men such as Fan Ju and Cai Ze.

sentences.zh: 注[四]韓子李斯曰「韓非飾辯詐謀，以釣利於秦」也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: Han Feizi and Li Si said, "Han Fei adorned disputation and deceitful schemes to hook profit in Qin."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: as Han Fei's school and Li Si put it, Han Fei "varnished clever argument and cunning plots to angle for gain in Qin."

sentences.zh: 賈誼過秦曰「贏糧而景從」也。

translations.literal: Jia Yi's Faulting Qin says "carrying grain on the back and following like a shadow."
translations.idiomatic: Jia Yi's Faulting Qin speaks of men who "shouldered dry rations and followed like a shadow."

sentences.zh: 及漢祖杖□，武夫□興，憲令寬賒，文禮簡闊，緒余四豪之烈，人懷陵上之心，[一]輕死重氣，怨惠必讎，令行私庭，權移匹庶，任俠之方，成其俗矣。

translations.literal: When the Han founder leaned on [weapon?], military men rose; statutes and orders were broad and slack, pattern and ritual simple and loose; the afterglow of the four heroes' fierceness remained, and people harbored hearts of overriding their superiors, [1] treating death lightly and prizing spirit, repaying kindness and injury alike with vengeance; commands issued from private courts, power shifted to commoners; the ways of entrusted chivalry became their custom.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Han founder, who took the realm with sword in hand, a breed of fighting men came to the fore; laws grew lax and ceremony thin, yet the glamour of the four great patrons of retainers still hung in the air. Men nursed ambitions to climb over their betters, [1] held life cheap and honor dear, and answered every slight with blood feud. Orders were obeyed in private mansions rather than at court, and real power slipped into the hands of commoners until the ethos of the knight-errant had woven itself into the fabric of society.

sentences.zh: [二] 自武帝以後，崇尚儒學，懷經協術，所在霧會，至有石渠分爭之論，黨同伐異之說，守文之徒，盛於時矣。

translations.literal: [2] From Emperor Wu onward, Confucian learning was honored; clutching the classics and harmonizing arts, they gathered like fog wherever they were; there reached the point of the Stone Canal's divided disputations, the discourse of banding with the same and attacking the different; the crowd who guarded the letter of the text flourished in the age.
translations.idiomatic: [2] After Emperor Wu, Confucian scholarship became the fashion; men who hugged the classics and brandished technical expertise flocked together everywhere. Debates at the Stone Canal Pavilion split schools against one another, "ally with your own kind and smite the rest" became a habit, and the literalists who clung to the written word dominated the age.

sentences.zh: [三]至王莽專偽，終於篡國，忠義之流，恥見纓紼，遂乃榮華丘壑，甘足枯槁。

translations.literal: [3] When Wang Mang monopolized hypocrisy and ended by usurping the state, the class of the loyal and righteous were ashamed to be seen with cap-strings and official cords; thereupon they took glory in hills and ravines and willingly endured withering away.
translations.idiomatic: [3] When Wang Mang's counterfeit virtue gave way to outright usurpation, men of loyalty and principle blushed to wear the hat-strings of office; many chose renown in mountain seclusion and accepted a life of dry austerity rather than serve him.

sentences.zh: [四]雖中興在運，漢德重開，而保身懷方，彌相慕襲，去就之節，重於時矣。

translations.literal: [4] Though restoration came by fate and Han virtue reopened, yet preserving the person and cherishing rectitude were ever more mutually emulated; the integrity of going or staying weighed heavily in the times.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Even after fate restored the Han and imperial virtue stirred again, the ideal of guarding one's integrity and holding fast to principle was more admired than ever; the choice whether to serve or withdraw had become the measure of a man.

sentences.zh: [五] 逮桓靈之閒，主荒政繆，國命委於閹寺，士子羞與為伍，故匹夫抗憤，處士橫議，遂乃激揚名聲，互相題拂，品核公卿，裁量執政，婞直之風，於斯行矣。

translations.literal: [5] By the time between Huan and Ling, the ruler was dissolute and government awry; the state's mandate was entrusted to eunuch temples, and gentlemen were ashamed to rank with them; therefore commoners roused indignation, recluses spoke out recklessly; thereupon they stirred and raised reputation, mutually inscribed and brushed one another, appraised dukes and ministers, measured those in power; the wind of stubborn uprightness ran thus.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Between Emperors Huan and Ling, the throne idled while policy ran off the rails, and the life of the state was handed to the palace eunuchs. Educated men loathed sharing their company, so ordinary citizens vented their outrage and recluses aired sharp opinions until students and scholars began trading praise, passing judgment on high ministers and weighing the conduct of those in power—an unbending moralism that now swept the land.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]四豪謂信陵君魏公子無忌、平原君趙勝、春申君黃歇、孟嘗君田文。

translations.literal: [6] Comment [1]: the four heroes mean Lord Xinling, Prince Wuji of Wei; Lord Pingyuan, Zhao Sheng; Lord Chunshen, Huang Xie; Lord Mengchang, Tian Wen.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Note [1]: the "four heroes" are Lord Xinling (Wei Wuji), Lord Pingyuan (Zhao Sheng), Lord Chunshen (Huang Xie), and Lord Mengchang (Tian Wen).

sentences.zh: 前書班固曰：「游談者以四豪為稱首。」

translations.literal: Ban Gu in the former book said, "Wandering talkers took the four heroes as their chief title."
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu observed in the Hanshu that wandering persuaders "ranked the four patrons foremost in their boasting."

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書音義曰：「相與信為任，同是非為俠，所謂權行州域，力折公侯者也。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the former book's Sounds and Meanings says, "Mutual trust is ren; same right and wrong is xia—what is called authority running through provinces and domains, force bending dukes and marquises."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: the Hanshu yinyi defines the knight-errant ethos as "keeping faith with friends is 'bearing responsibility'; sharing the same verdict on right and wrong is 'chivalry'—the type who throws weight across whole regions and can humble even nobles with sheer force of character."

sentences.zh: 注[三]武帝詔求賢良，於是公孫弘、董仲舒等出焉。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Emperor Wu issued an edict seeking worthy and good men; thereupon Gongsun Hong, Dong Zhongshu, and others came forth.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Emperor Wu's call for worthy men brought forth figures such as Gongsun Hong and Dong Zhongshu.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，集諸儒於石渠閣，講論六蓺。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time, they assembled the various Confucians at the Stone Canal Pavilion and discussed the six arts.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan the court gathered Confucian scholars at the Stone Canal Pavilion to debate the six classical arts.

sentences.zh: 召五經名儒太子蕭望之等大議殿中，平公羊、谷梁同異，同己者朋黨之，異己者攻伐之。

translations.literal: They summoned famous Confucians of the Five Classics, the heir apparent Xiao Wangzhi, and others to debate greatly in the palace, adjudicating Gongyang and Guliang sameness and difference; those same as themselves they made into factions; those different from themselves they attacked.
translations.idiomatic: Noted masters of the Five Classics, including the heir apparent's tutor Xiao Wangzhi, were summoned to the palace to settle the quarrel between the Gongyang and Guliang traditions; scholars who agreed banded together, while those who disagreed were set upon.

sentences.zh: 劉歆書曰：「黨同門，妒道真。」

translations.literal: Liu Xin's letter says, "They banded with those of the same gate and envied the true way."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin complained that they "clung to classmates and begrudged anyone who held the genuine teaching."

sentences.zh: 注[四]謂襲勝、薛方、郭欽、蔣詡之類，並隱居不應莽召。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: it means the likes of Gong Sheng, Xue Fang, Guo Qin, and Jiang Xu—together they hid in reclusion and did not respond to Mang's summons.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Gong Sheng, Xue Fang, Guo Qin, Jiang Xu, and their like withdrew to seclusion and ignored Wang Mang's calls to office.

sentences.zh: 注[五]謂逢萌、嚴光、周黨、尚長之屬。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: it means the class of Feng Meng, Yan Guang, Zhou Dang, Shang Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Feng Meng, Yan Guang, Zhou Dang, Shang Chang, and similar men who shunned the Later Han court.

sentences.zh: 注[六]婞，狠也，音邢鼎反。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: xing (婞) means fierce; the reading is the xing-ding fan cut (邢鼎反).
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: xing (婞) means stubborn or harsh; read it with the xing-ding fanqie.

sentences.zh: 夫上好則下必甚，矯枉故直必過，其理然矣。

translations.literal: When those above love something, those below are sure to carry it to excess; in straightening a crook one necessarily overshoots the straight—such is the pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Whatever the ruler favors, the people will push to extremes; bend a bow too far the other way and you overshoot the mark—that is simply how things work.

sentences.zh: [一]若范滂、張儉之徒，清心忌惡，終陷黨議，不其然乎？

translations.literal: [1] As for the likes of Fan Pang and Zhang Jian, who purified their hearts and hated evil, yet in the end fell into partisan prosecution—is it not so?
translations.idiomatic: [1] Think of Fan Pang and Zhang Jian, men who kept their hearts clean and loathed wickedness, yet still ended in the coils of the partisan proscriptions—was that not inevitable?

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮記曰：「下之事上也，不從其所令，從其所行。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Record of Rites says, "In what is below serving what is above, they do not follow what he commands, they follow what he practices.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Liji says, "Those below do not mirror their superiors' orders so much as their superiors' deeds."

sentences.zh: 上好是物，下必有甚者矣。

translations.literal: What those above prize as a thing, those below are sure to carry to excess.
translations.idiomatic: Whatever the ruler singles out for praise, the people will pursue to fanatical lengths.

sentences.zh: 」矯，正也。

translations.literal: Jiao means to correct.
translations.idiomatic: Jiao means to set something straight.

sentences.zh: 正枉必過其直，見孟子。

translations.literal: Straightening the crooked necessarily passes beyond the straight—see Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: Mencius observes that in straightening a bend you inevitably overshoot the true line.

sentences.zh: 初，桓帝為蠡吾侯，受學於甘陵周福，及即帝位，擢福為尚書。

translations.literal: At first, when Emperor Huan was marquis of Liwu, he received instruction from Zhou Fu of Ganling; when he ascended the imperial throne, he promoted Fu to secretary of the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: In his youth, while still marquis of Liwu, Emperor Huan had studied under Zhou Fu of Ganling; once he took the throne he raised Zhou Fu to the post of imperial secretary.

sentences.zh: 時同郡河南尹房植有名當朝，鄉人為之謠曰：「天下規矩房伯武，因師獲印周仲進。

translations.literal: At that time Fang Zhi, intendant of Henan of the same commandery, had fame at court; the villagers made a ditty for them, saying, "Under heaven the compass and square—Fang Bowu; through the teacher gaining the seal—Zhou Zhongjin.
translations.idiomatic: Fang Zhi of the same commandery, then serving as governor of Henan, was a leading name at court, and local folk sang: "Fang Bowu sets the standard for the realm; Zhou Zhongjin owes his seal to his teacher."

sentences.zh: 」二家賓客，互相譏揣，[一]遂各樹朋徒，漸成尤隙，由是甘陵有南北部，黨人之議，自此始矣。

translations.literal: " The retainers of the two houses mutually ridiculed and probed; [1] thereupon each planted factions of followers, and gradually enmity and rifts formed; from this Ganling had south and north divisions—the discourse of "partisans" began from this.
translations.idiomatic: " The clients of the two camps traded barbs until each side recruited its own following and bad blood hardened; Ganling split into rival "southern" and "northern" cliques, and talk of "factions" dates from that moment."

sentences.zh: 後汝南太守宗資任功曹范滂，南陽太守成□亦委功曹岑晊，[二]二郡又為謠曰：

translations.literal: Later the governor of Runan, Zong Zi, entrusted affairs to his merit assessor Fan Pang; the governor of Nanyang, Cheng [Jin], also delegated to his merit assessor Cen Zhi; [2] the two commanderies again made a ditty, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Later Zong Zi, governor of Runan, left real authority to his merit assessor Fan Pang, while Cheng Jin of Nanyang did the same with Cen Zhi; [2] the two regions circulated another rhyme:

sentences.zh: 「汝南太守范孟博，南陽宗資主畫諾。

translations.literal: "The governor of Runan is Fan Mengbo; Zong Zi of Nanyang only marks approval.
translations.idiomatic: "Fan Mengbo runs Runan; in Nanyang Zong Zi merely initials what Fan has already decided."

sentences.zh: 南陽太守岑公孝，弘農成□但坐嘯。

translations.literal: The governor of Nanyang is Cen Gongxiao; Cheng [Jin] of Hongnong only sits and whistles.
translations.idiomatic: "Cen Gongxiao runs Nanyang; Cheng Jin of Hongnong need only lean back and whistle while Cen runs the show."

sentences.zh: 」[三] 因此流言轉入太學，諸生三萬餘人，郭林宗、賈偉節為其冠，[四]並與李膺、陳蕃、王暢更相□重。

translations.literal: " [3] From this rumor flowed into the Imperial Academy; the students, more than thirty thousand, with Guo Linzong and Jia Weijie as their chief, [4] together with Li Ying, Chen Fan, and Wang Chang mutually [esteemed] one another.
translations.idiomatic: " [3] The gossip soon reached the Imperial Academy, where more than thirty thousand students looked to Guo Tai (Linzong) and Jia Biao (Weijie) as their leaders, [4] and together with Li Ying, Chen Fan, and Wang Chang they traded unstinting praise."

sentences.zh: 學中語曰：「天下模楷李元禮，不畏強禦陳仲舉，天下俊秀王叔茂。

translations.literal: A saying in the Academy went, "Under heaven the model and pattern—Li Yuanli; not fearing the powerful—Chen Zhongju; under heaven the outstanding talent—Wang Shumao.
translations.idiomatic: A student slogan ran: "Li Ying (Yuanli) is the measure of the age; Chen Fan (Zhongju) fears no bully; Wang Chang (Shumao) is the finest mind in the land."

sentences.zh: 」又渤海公族進階、[五]扶風魏齊卿，並危言深論，不隱豪強。

translations.literal: " Again Gongzu Jinjie of Bohai, [5] Wei Qiqing of Fufeng, together spoke perilous words and deep disquisitions, not concealing from powerful magnates.
translations.idiomatic: " Gongzu Jinjie of Bohai [5] and Wei Qiqing of Fufeng joined them, airing blunt truths and searching critiques without sparing the mighty."

sentences.zh: [六] 自公卿以下，莫不畏其貶議，屣履到門。

translations.literal: [6] From dukes and ministers downward, none did not fear their disparaging criticism; they dragged slippers and came to their gates.
translations.idiomatic: [6] From the highest ministers on down, everyone dreaded their censure; notables would hurry to their doors in disheveled haste, slippers half on.

sentences.zh: 注[一]初委反。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the reading is the chu-wei fan cut (初委反).
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: read with the chu-wei fanqie gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[二]音質。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the reading is like zhi (質).
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: pronounce like zhi (質).

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰「成□少修仁義，篤學，以清名見。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Xie Cheng's book says, "Cheng [Jin] in youth cultivated benevolence and righteousness, studied deeply, and was seen for his pure reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Xie Cheng records that Cheng Jin from boyhood pursued benevolence and duty, studied with single-minded zeal, and won notice for an unsullied name.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，拜郎中，遷南陽太守。

translations.literal: He was recommended as filial and incorrupt, appointed gentleman of the palace, promoted to governor of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and incorrupt, he entered the palace corps and rose to governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 郡舊多豪強，中官黃門盤*

translations.literal: The commandery had long had many powerful families; eunuchs of the Yellow Gate coiled*
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang had always bristled with powerful houses, while palace eunuchs of the Yellow Gate were tangled*

translations.literal: (ya [fang/teeth])
translations.idiomatic: (the gloss ya (牙) suggests interlocking "fang-teeth," i.e. mutually entrenched power)

translations.literal: **[mutually]* boundaries.
translations.idiomatic: with them across local boundaries—the text is damaged here.)

sentences.zh: □下車，振威嚴以撿攝之。

translations.literal: [Cheng Jin] on taking office, roused awesome dignity to inspect and restrain them.
translations.idiomatic: When Cheng Jin took up his post, he moved at once to awe the clans into order and bring them under control.

sentences.zh: 是時桓帝乳母、中官貴人外親張子禁，怙恃貴埶，不畏法網，功曹岑晊勸使捕子禁付宛獄，笞殺之。

translations.literal: At this time the wet nurse of Emperor Huan, Zhang Zijin, a kinsman by marriage of a favored eunuch of the palace, relied on noble influence, feared no snare of law; merit assessor Cen Zhi urged that Zijin be seized and delivered to the Wan prison, where he was flogged to death.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zijin, the wet nurse's kin tied by marriage to an influential inner-court eunuch, swaggered above the law until Cen Zhi, the merit assessor, had him dragged to the Wan jail and beaten to death.

sentences.zh: 桓帝征□，下獄死。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan summoned [Cheng Jin, name defective in text]; he was cast into prison and died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Governor Cheng to the capital; he never left prison alive.

sentences.zh: 宗資字叔都，南陽安觿人也。

translations.literal: Zong Zi, courtesy name Shudu, was a man of Anxi in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zong Zi, known as Shudu, came from Anxi in Nanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: 家代為漢將相名臣。

translations.literal: For generations his family had been celebrated ministers and generals of Han.
translations.idiomatic: His house had supplied noted generals and chief ministers to the Han for generations.

sentences.zh: 祖父均，自有傳。

translations.literal: His grandfather Jun has his own biography.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Zong Jun has a separate biography in this history.

sentences.zh: 資少在京師，學孟氏易、歐陽尚書。

translations.literal: In youth Zi was in the capital, studying the Meng family's Changes and the Ouyang Documents.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man in the capital he mastered the Meng commentary on the Changes and the Ouyang recension of the Documents.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，拜議郎，補御史中丞、汝南太守。

translations.literal: He was recommended filial and incorrupt, appointed gentleman consultant, then supplemented as imperial secretary and governor of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and incorrupt, he rose through the consultant corps to imperial secretary and finally governor of Runan.

sentences.zh: 署范滂為功曹，委任政事，推功於滂，不伐其美。

translations.literal: He appointed Fan Pang merit assessor, entrusted government affairs, pushed credit onto Pang, and did not boast of his own excellence.
translations.idiomatic: He named Fan Pang merit assessor, handed him the reins of administration, and always credited Fan for successes while refusing to advertise his own part.

sentences.zh: 任善之名，聞於海內」也。

translations.literal: The fame of entrusting good men was heard within the seas."
translations.idiomatic: Thus he won a reputation, heard across the realm, for trusting able men."

sentences.zh: 注[四]冠猶首也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: the word glossed as cap means head, that is, foremost.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: here it means they stood at the head of the company.

sentences.zh: 注[五]公族，姓也，名進階。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: Gongzu is the surname, the given name is Jinjie.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Gongzu is the clan name and Jinjie the personal name.

sentences.zh: 風俗通曰：「晉成公立嫡子為公族大夫。

translations.literal: The Comprehensive Meaning of Customs and Mores says, "Duke Cheng of Jin established his heir son as grandee of the ducal clan.
translations.idiomatic: The Fengsu tongyi records that Duke Cheng of Jin appointed his heir-apparent grandee of the ducal kindred."

sentences.zh: 」韓無忌號公族穆子，見左氏傳。

translations.literal: " Han Wuji was titled grandee Muzhi of the ducal clan—see the Zuo Commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Han Wuji bore the title Grandee Muzhi of the ducal clan; the Zuo Commentary tells his story."

sentences.zh: 注[六]危言謂不畏危難而直言也。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: dangerous speech means not fearing peril and difficulty but speaking straight.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: the gloss takes wei yan as blunt speech that does not flinch from risk.

sentences.zh: 論語孔子曰：「邦有道，危言危行。」

translations.literal: The Analects: Confucius said, "When the state has the Way, there are dangerous words and dangerous deeds."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius says in the Analects that when the realm is well ordered, one may speak and act boldly."

sentences.zh: 時河內張成善說風角，推占當赦，遂教子殺人。

translations.literal: At the time Zhang Cheng of Henei was skilled in expounding wind-angles divination; he calculated by divination that an amnesty was due, and thereupon taught his son to commit murder.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cheng of Henei, a master of wind-angles astrology, foretold an impending amnesty and urged his son to commit murder in the belief the boy would walk free.

sentences.zh: 李膺為河南尹，督促收捕，既而逢宥獲免，膺愈懷憤疾，竟案殺之。

translations.literal: Li Ying was intendant of Henan; he pressed for seizure and arrest; afterward the culprit met pardon and escaped; Ying harbored still more indignant hatred and in the end investigated and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying, as governor of Henan, pressed for the killer's arrest, but an amnesty spared him. Li Ying's rage only deepened until he had the man tried and put to death anyway.

sentences.zh: 初，成以方伎交通宦官，帝亦頗誶其占。

translations.literal: At first Cheng, by occult arts, had dealings with eunuchs; the emperor also somewhat ridiculed his divination.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cheng had long curried favor with the eunuchs through his arts, and the emperor himself had begun to scoff at his prophecies.

sentences.zh: 成弟子牢修因上書誣告膺等養太學游士，交結諸郡生徒，更相驅馳，共為部黨，誹訕朝廷，疑亂風俗。

translations.literal: Zhang Cheng's disciple Lao Xiu thereupon submitted a memorial falsely accusing Ying and others of supporting wandering scholars of the Imperial Academy, associating with students of the various commanderies, mutually spurring one another on, together forming factional cliques, slandering the court, and casting doubt on and disordering customs.
translations.idiomatic: His pupil Lao Xiu then memorialized the throne, charging Li Ying and his circle with harboring dissident scholars from the Imperial Academy, networking with students across the commanderies, dashing about in one another's cause, forming a clandestine league, vilifying the government, and unsettling public morals.

sentences.zh: [一]於是天子震怒，班下郡國，逮捕黨人，佈告天下，使同忿疾，遂收執膺等。

translations.literal: [1] Thereupon the Son of Heaven was shaken with wrath; he issued orders to the commanderies and kingdoms, arrested the partisans, promulgated notice throughout the realm to make all share indignant hatred, and thereupon seized and took Ying and others.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The emperor exploded with rage, sent warrants to every province, rounded up the so-called partisans, and published their names so the whole country would turn on them; Li Ying and the first wave were thrown in chains.

sentences.zh: 其辭所連及陳寔之徒二百餘人，或有逃遁不獲，皆懸金購募。

translations.literal: Those whom the wording implicated, such as Chen Shi, were more than two hundred persons; some fled and were not caught—all had gold hung for their capture and recruitment.
translations.idiomatic: Over two hundred men, including Chen Shi, were named in the indictment; fugitives carried prices on their heads in gold.

sentences.zh: 使者四出，相望於道。

translations.literal: Envoys went out in the four directions, gazing at one another on the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Couriers fanned out until their relay teams seemed to meet on every highway.

sentences.zh: 明年，尚書霍諝、城門校尉竇武並表為請，帝意稍解，乃皆赦歸田裡，禁錮終身。

translations.literal: The next year Minister Secretary Huo Xu and colonel of the city gates Dou Wu jointly submitted memorials pleading for them; the emperor's intent slightly relaxed, and thereupon all were pardoned to return to their fields and hamlets, with lifelong proscription.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Huo Xu and Dou Wu petitioned together; the emperor relented enough to release the prisoners to their homes but barred them from office for life.

sentences.zh: 而黨人之名，猶書王府。

translations.literal: Yet the names of the partisans were still written in the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Their names nonetheless stayed on file in the imperial archives.

sentences.zh: 注[一]說文曰：「誹，謗也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Shuowen says, fei means slander.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Shuowen defines fei as slander.

sentences.zh: 」蒼頡篇曰：「訕，非也。」

translations.literal: The Cangjie primer says, shan means wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The Cangjie pian glosses shan as to condemn or reject.

sentences.zh: 自是正直廢放，邪枉熾結，海內希風之流，遂共相摽搒，[一]指天下名士，為之稱號。

translations.literal: From this time the upright were cast aside and banished; the crooked blazed and knotted; those within the seas who hoped in the wind thereupon mutually raised placards, [1] pointing to famous gentlemen of the realm and making titles for them.
translations.idiomatic: With honest men ruined and villains ascendant, every climber who sniffed the breeze began trading lists, [1] pinning honorific tags on celebrated scholars across the land.

sentences.zh: 上曰「三君」，次曰「八俊」，次曰「八顧」，次曰「八及」，次曰「八廚」，猶古之「八元」、「八凱」也。

translations.literal: The highest were called the "Three Lords"; next the "Eight Handsome"; next the "Eight who Look Back"; next the "Eight who Reach"; next the "Eight Kitchens"—like the ancient "Eight Yuan" and "Eight Kai."
translations.idiomatic: They ranked cliques as the Three Lords, then the Eight Handsome, the Eight Patrons, the Eight Links, and the Eight Kitchens, aping the old legends of the Eight Yuan and Eight Kai.

sentences.zh: 竇武、劉淑、陳蕃為「三君」。

translations.literal: Dou Wu, Liu Shu, and Chen Fan were the "Three Lords."
translations.idiomatic: Dou Wu, Liu Shu, and Chen Fan headed the list as the Three Lords.

sentences.zh: 君者，言一世之所宗也。

translations.literal: Lord means what a generation takes as its object of reverence.
translations.idiomatic: "Lord" here means the men an entire age looked up to.

sentences.zh: 李膺、荀翌、杜密、王暢、劉祐、魏朗、趙典、朱□為「八俊」。

translations.literal: Li Ying, Xu Yi, Du Mi, Wang Chang, Liu You, Wei Lang, Zhao Dian, and Zhu [name missing] were the "Eight Handsome."
translations.idiomatic: The Eight Handsome roster listed Li Ying, Xu Yi, Du Mi, Wang Chang, Liu You, Wei Lang, Zhao Dian, and Zhu—whose full name the manuscript omits.

sentences.zh: 俊者，言人之英也。

translations.literal: Handsome means the flower of men.
translations.idiomatic: "Handsome" marks them as the age's most brilliant men.

sentences.zh: 郭林宗、宗慈、巴肅、夏馥、范滂、尹勳、蔡衍、羊陟為「八顧」。

translations.literal: Guo Linzong, Zong Ci, Ba Su, Xia Fu, Fan Pang, Yin Xun, Cai Yan, and Yang Zhi were the "Eight who Look Back."
translations.idiomatic: The Eight Patrons were Guo Tai (Linzong), Zong Ci, Ba Su, Xia Fu, Fan Pang, Yin Xun, Cai Yan, and Yang Zhi.

sentences.zh: 顧者，言能以德行引人者也。

translations.literal: Look-back means those able by virtue and conduct to lead others.
translations.idiomatic: "Patron" here means men whose virtue drew others after them.

sentences.zh: 張儉、岑晊、劉表、陳翔、孔昱、苑康、□*

translations.literal: Zhang Jian, Cen Zhi, Liu Biao, Chen Xiang, Kong Yu, Yuan Kang, [name missing]*
translations.idiomatic: The Eight Links began with Zhang Jian, Cen Zhi, Liu Biao, Chen Xiang, Kong Yu, Yuan Kang, and a name lost in the lacuna—

translations.literal: (fu [gloss])
translations.idiomatic: —where the manuscript gloss suggests the missing graph may read fu.

translations.literal: **[lacuna]*, Zhai Chao were the "Eight who Reach."
translations.idiomatic: —together with Zhai Chao, completing the roll called the Eight Links.

sentences.zh: 及者，言其能導人追宗者也。

translations.literal: Reach means those able to guide people to pursue what they revere.
translations.idiomatic: "Link" means they could pull others toward the leaders they admired.

sentences.zh: [二]度尚、張邈、王考、劉儒、胡母班、秦周、蕃向、王章為「八廚」。

translations.literal: [2] Du Shang, Zhang Miao, Wang Kao, Liu Ru, Humu Ban, Qin Zhou, Fan Xiang, and Wang Zhang were the "Eight Kitchens."
translations.idiomatic: [2] The Eight Kitchens were Du Shang, Zhang Miao, Wang Kao, Liu Ru, Humu Ban, Qin Zhou, Fan Xiang, and Wang Zhang.

sentences.zh: [三]廚者，言能以財救人者也。

translations.literal: [3] Kitchen means those able by wealth to rescue people.
translations.idiomatic: [3] "Kitchen" referred to hosts who fed and funded fugitives.

sentences.zh: 注[一]希，望也，摽搒猶相稱揚也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: xi means hope; biao bang is like mutually praising and raising.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: xi means to look toward; biao bang means they trumpeted one another's names.

sentences.zh: 「搒」與「牓」同，古字通。

translations.literal: "Bang" is the same as "bang [placard]"; in ancient graphs they interchange.
translations.idiomatic: The character here is the same word later written with the placard graph; the forms were interchangeable in antiquity.

sentences.zh: 注[二]導，引也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: dao means to lead.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: dao means to draw someone along.

sentences.zh: 宗謂所宗仰者。

translations.literal: Zong means those whom one reveres and looks up to.
translations.idiomatic: Zong here means the figurehead whom followers revered.

sentences.zh: 注[三]蕃，姓也，音皮。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Fan is the surname; the commentary gives a pi reading tied to the word for hide.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Fan is a clan name; pronounce it with the pi initial suggested in the gloss.

sentences.zh: 又張儉鄉人朱並，承望中常侍侯覽意旨，上書告儉與同鄉二十四人別相署號，共為部黨，圖危社稷。

translations.literal: Again Zhang Jian's townsman Zhu Bing, looking to the intent of the regular attendant Hou Lan, submitted a memorial accusing Jian and twenty-four men of the same township of separately signing titles for one another, together forming a factional clique, plotting peril to the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Bing, a fellow townsman of Zhang Jian, currying favor with the eunuch Hou Lan, accused Zhang and twenty-four neighbors of inventing factional titles and conspiring against the state.

sentences.zh: 以儉及□彬、褚鳳、張肅、薛蘭、馮禧、魏玄、徐干為「八俊」，田林、張隱、劉表、薛郁、王訪、劉祗、宣靖、公緒恭為「八顧」，[一] 朱楷、田盤、□耽、薛敦、宋布、唐龍、嬴咨、宣□為「八及」，刻石立墠，共為部黨，而儉為之魁。

translations.literal: Taking Jian and [Ruan?] Bin, Chu Feng, Zhang Su, Xue Lan, Feng Xi, Wei Xuan, Xu Gan as the "Eight Handsome"; Tian Lin, Zhang Yin, Liu Biao, Xue Yu, Wang Fang, Liu Zhi, Xuan Jing, Gongxu Gong as the "Eight who Look Back," [1] Zhu Kai, Tian Pan, [Dan?] Dan, Xue Dun, Song Bu, Tang Long, Ying Zi, Xuan [lacuna] as the "Eight who Reach"—they carved stone and set up a shan altar, together forming a factional clique, and Jian was their chief.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The indictment split Zhang Jian's circle into new Eight Handsome, Eight Patron, and Eight Link lists—some names garbled in the stone—erected a sworn stele on a cleared ritual plot, and named Zhang Jian ringleader.

sentences.zh: [二]靈帝詔刊章捕儉等。

translations.literal: [2] Emperor Ling issued an edict to shave the text from the document and arrest Jian and others.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Emperor Ling ordered the names struck from the published warrant and Zhang Jian's band hunted down.

sentences.zh: [三]大長秋曹節因此諷有司奏捕前黨故司空虞放、太僕杜密、長樂少府李膺、司隸校尉朱□、穎川太守巴肅、沛相荀翌、河內太守魏朗、山陽太守翟超、任城相劉儒、太尉掾范滂等百餘人，皆死獄中。

translations.literal: [3] The grand prolonger of autumn Cao Jie thereupon indirectly prompted the responsible offices to memorialize arresting former partisans: former minister of works Yu Fang, grand coachman Du Mi, chamberlain for the palace of Changlo Li Ying, metropolitan commandant Zhu [lacuna], governor of Yingchuan Ba Su, chancellor of Pei Xu Yi, governor of Henei Wei Lang, governor of Shanyang Zhai Chao, chancellor of Rencheng Liu Ru, steward to the grand commandant Fan Pang, more than a hundred persons—all died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Cao Jie, the ranking eunuch, then had the bureaus rearrest over a hundred earlier "partisans," from the retired minister Yu Fang down to Fan Pang, the grand commandant's aide; every one of them perished in jail.

sentences.zh: 余或先歿不及，或亡命獲免。

translations.literal: Of the rest, some died beforehand and did not reach the net, or fled their lives and obtained escape.
translations.idiomatic: Others had already died or slipped the dragnet as fugitives.

sentences.zh: 自此諸為怨隙者，因相陷害，睚□之忿，濫入黨中。

translations.literal: From this time those who bore grudges and rifts, by means of mutual framing and injury, resentments of a sidelong glance's width were recklessly entered into the partisans.
translations.idiomatic: After that anyone nursing a grudge could denounce a rival; the slightest squabble might land a man on the partisan list.

sentences.zh: [四]又州郡承旨，或有未嘗交關，亦離禍毒。

translations.literal: [4] Again the commanderies and kingdoms received intent; some had never had dealings, yet also suffered calamity and poison.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Local officials, eager to obey hints from above, ruined men who had never even met the accused.

sentences.zh: 其死徙廢禁者，六七百人。

translations.literal: Those who died, were banished, dismissed, or proscribed were six or seven hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: Death, exile, dismissal, or lifetime ban touched six or seven hundred souls.

sentences.zh: 注[一]公緒，姓也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Gongxu is the surname.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Gongxu is a compound surname.

sentences.zh: 注[二]墠，除地於中為□。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: a shan is cleared ground in the middle for a ritual platform; the following graph is missing.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: a shan is a swept ritual plot; the lacuna likely names the earthen platform.

sentences.zh: 墠音禪。

translations.literal: The commentary gloss pairs the graph with the word for abdication in the same spelling family.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss indicates a chan-type reading for the graph shan.

sentences.zh: 魁，大帥也。

translations.literal: The word kui means great leader.
translations.idiomatic: Kui means ringleader.

sentences.zh: 注[三]刊，削。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: kan means to shave or cut away.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: kan means to scrape away, here the names on the warrant.

sentences.zh: 不欲宣露並名，故削除之，而直捕儉等。

translations.literal: Not wishing to publish and expose all the names, therefore they shaved them away and directly arrested Jian and others.
translations.idiomatic: The court struck the co-conspirators from the edict so only Zhang Jian's band would be hunted.

sentences.zh: 注[四]睚音五懈反。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: the commentary gives a wu-xie fanqie reading for the graph ya.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: pronounce ya with the wu-xie fanqie.

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「睚，裂也。

translations.literal: The Guangya says, "Ya means split, rend.
translations.idiomatic: The Guangya glosses ya as a rending glance, a hair-trigger feud.

sentences.zh: 」□音才賜反。

translations.literal: The lacunose graph is read with the cai-ci fanqie gloss.
translations.idiomatic: The second character of the compound is read with the cai-ci fanqie.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「瞋目魍也。

translations.literal: The former book's Sounds and Meanings says, "Glaring eyes [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu yinyi defines the compound as a glare that promises revenge.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰：「睚□之隙必報。」

translations.literal: " The Records of the Historian says, "For a crevice of sidelong-glance width, repayment is sure."
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji warns that even a sidelong glance can demand blood payment.

sentences.zh: 熹平五年，永昌太守曹鸞上書大訟黨人，言甚方切。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Xiping, Cao Luan, governor of Yongchang, submitted a memorial greatly arguing the case of the partisans; his words were very forthright and cutting.
translations.idiomatic: In 176 the governor of Yongchang, Cao Luan, sent a blunt memorial pleading the partisans' innocence.

sentences.zh: 帝省奏大怒，即詔司隸、益州檻車收鸞，送槐裡獄掠殺之。

translations.literal: The emperor, reviewing the memorial, was greatly angry; immediately he edicted the metropolitan commandant and Yi Province to take Luan in a cage cart, send him to Huaili prison, torture and kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling read it and flew into a rage, ordering Luan seized in a prison cart, dragged to Huaili, and tortured to death.

sentences.zh: 於是又詔州郡更考黨人門生故吏父子兄弟，其在位者，免官禁錮，爰及五屬。

translations.literal: Thereupon he again edicted the commanderies and kingdoms to re-investigate partisans' disciples, former subordinates, fathers and sons, brothers; those in office were dismissed and proscribed, extending to the five kin grades.
translations.idiomatic: He then commanded a fresh sweep of every partisan's students, old staff, and male kin through five degrees of mourning; any who held office lost it and faced lifelong disqualification.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]謂斬衰、齊衰、大功、小功、緦麻也。

translations.literal: [1] Comment [1]: it means zhui cui, zi cui, da gong, xiao gong, si ma.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note [1]: the five grades are the unlined and hemmed year mourning, the nine-month and five-month shares, and the lightest hemp.

sentences.zh: 光和二年，上祿長和海[一]上言：「禮，從祖兄弟別居異財，恩義已輕，服屬□末。

translations.literal: In the second year of Guanghe, the magistrate of Shanglu, He Hai [1], submitted words: "By ritual, cousins once removed dwelling apart with separate property—the grace and duty are already light; the garment kinship is at the [lacuna] end.
translations.idiomatic: In 179 He Hai, magistrate of Shanglu, argued that ritual treats fourth-cousin kin who keep separate households as only lightly bound; their mourning ties barely register.

sentences.zh: 而今黨人錮及五族，既乖典訓之文，有謬經常之法。

translations.literal: Yet now the partisan proscription reaches five clans; it already violates the text of canonical instruction and errs against the law of constant norms.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the partisan ban still muzzled five collateral lines, contradicting canonical teaching and statutory common sense.

sentences.zh: 」[二]帝覽而悟之，黨錮自從祖以下，皆得解釋。

translations.literal: " [2] The emperor, reading, understood; from collateral great-granduncle downward in the partisan proscription, all obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The emperor took the point and lifted the ban for everyone beyond the third-cousin circle.

sentences.zh: 注[一]上祿，縣，屬武都郡，今成州縣也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Shanglu is a county belonging to Wudu commandery; it is now a county of Chengzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Shanglu county lay in Wudu, in the area of modern Chengzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]左氏傳曰：「父子兄弟，罪不相及。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the Zuo Commentary says, "Father and son, elder and younger brothers—guilt does not reach across."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: the Zuo Tradition insists that kin should not pay for one another's crimes.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，黃巾賊起，中常侍呂強言於帝曰：「黨錮久積，人情多怨。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping, the Yellow Turban bandits arose; the regular attendant Lü Qiang spoke to the emperor, saying, "The partisan proscription has long piled up; human feelings harbor much resentment.
translations.idiomatic: When the Yellow Turbans rose in 184, the eunuch Lü Qiang warned the throne that decades of partisan ostracism had stockpiled popular resentment.

sentences.zh: 若久不赦宥，輕與張角合謀，為變滋大，悔之無救。

translations.literal: If long there is no amnesty, they will lightly join Zhang Jiao in plotting; the rebellion will grow greater, and regret will not save.
translations.idiomatic: If you keep them under ban, they will drift toward Zhang Jiao's conspiracy until the uprising swells beyond remedy.

sentences.zh: 」帝懼其言，乃大赦黨人，誅徙之家皆歸故郡。

translations.literal: " The emperor feared his words and thereupon greatly pardoned the partisans; families of those executed or banished all returned to their former commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Alarmed, the emperor declared a general amnesty for the partisans and let exiled families go home.

sentences.zh: 其後黃巾遂盛，朝野崩離，綱紀文章蕩然矣。

translations.literal: Afterward the Yellow Turbans thereupon flourished; court and wilds collapsed and parted; cords and pattern, culture and order, were utterly gone.
translations.idiomatic: The rebellion still engulfed the realm; government and countryside split apart until law and letters alike dissolved.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]詩大雅蕩篇序曰：「厲王無道，天下蕩蕩，無綱紀文章。

translations.literal: [1] Comment [1]: the preface to the "Yao" poem in the Da ya of the Odes says, "King Li lacked the Way; all under heaven was vast and void; there were no cords and pattern, culture and order.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note [1]: the Great Ya preface on King Li describes a realm adrift, stripped of institutions and literacy.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「蕩蕩，法度廢壞之魍也。」

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan's commentary says, "Vast and void means the appearance of laws and standards ruined."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses the phrase as the look of a polity whose laws have rotted away.

sentences.zh: 凡黨事始自甘陵、汝南，成於李膺、張儉，海內塗炭，二十餘年，諸所蔓衍，皆天下善士。

translations.literal: In general the partisan affair began from Ganling and Runan, was completed with Li Ying and Zhang Jian; within the seas mud and charcoal for more than twenty years; wherever it spread and extended, all were good gentlemen of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: The whole tragedy began in Ganling and Runan, crested with Li Ying and Zhang Jian, and for twenty years scorched the empire; nearly everyone caught in its net was a man of virtue.

sentences.zh: 三君、八俊等三十五人，其名多存者，並載乎篇。

translations.literal: The Three Lords, Eight Handsome, and others, thirty-five men—those whose names mostly survive are together carried in this chapter.
translations.idiomatic: Thirty-five men from the ranked lists whose names survive are treated in the following notices.

sentences.zh: 陳蕃、竇武、王暢、劉表、度尚、郭林宗別有傳。

translations.literal: Chen Fan, Dou Wu, Wang Chang, Liu Biao, Du Shang, and Guo Linzong have separate biographies.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Fan, Dou Wu, Wang Chang, Liu Biao, Du Shang, and Guo Tai already have chapters of their own.

sentences.zh: 荀翌附祖淑傳。

translations.literal: Xu Yi is attached to the biography of his grandfather Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Yi appears with his grandfather Xun Shu.

sentences.zh: 張邈附呂布傳。

translations.literal: Zhang Miao is attached to Lü Bu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Miao is narrated alongside Lü Bu.

sentences.zh: 胡母班附袁紹傳。

translations.literal: Humu Ban is attached to Yuan Shao's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Humu Ban is treated in Yuan Shao's biography.

sentences.zh: 王考字文祖，東平壽張人，冀州刺史；

translations.literal: Wang Kao, courtesy name Wenju, a man of Shouzhang in Dongping—inspector of Ji province;
translations.idiomatic: Wang Kao (Wenju) of Shouzhang in Dongping served as inspector of Ji;

sentences.zh: 秦周字平王，陳留平丘人，北海相；

translations.literal: Qin Zhou, courtesy name Pingwang, a man of Pingqiu in Chenliu—chancellor of Beihai;
translations.idiomatic: Qin Zhou (Pingwang) of Pingqiu in Chenliu was chancellor of Beihai;

sentences.zh: 蕃向字嘉景，魯國人，郎中；

translations.literal: Fan Xiang, courtesy name Jiajing, a man of Lu—gentleman of the palace;
translations.idiomatic: Fan Xiang (Jiajing) of Lu held a gentleman-of-the-palace appointment;

sentences.zh: 王璋字伯儀，東萊曲城人，少府卿：[一]位行並不顯。

translations.literal: Wang Zhang, courtesy name Boyi, a man of Qucheng in Donglai—minister coachman: [1] rank and conduct were both not illustrious.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Zhang (Boyi) of Qucheng in Donglai rose to minister coachman, [1] yet left little mark in office.

sentences.zh: 翟超，山陽太守，事見陳蕃傳，字及郡縣未詳。

translations.literal: Zhai Chao, governor of Shanyang—the affair is seen in Chen Fan's biography; courtesy name and commandery-county are not detailed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Chao, governor of Shanyang, figures in Chen Fan's chapter; his style name and native place are unknown.

sentences.zh: 朱□，沛人，與杜密等俱死獄中。

translations.literal: Zhu [lacuna], a man of Pei—together with Du Mi and others died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu of Pei died in jail with Du Mi and the rest.

sentences.zh: 唯趙典名見而已。

translations.literal: Only Zhao Dian's name appears—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhao Dian rates a bare mention elsewhere.

sentences.zh: 注[一]曲城，縣，故城在今萊州掖縣東北也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Qucheng is a county; its old walled city is northeast of Ye county in Laizhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: old Qucheng lay northeast of present Ye county in Shandong.

sentences.zh: 劉淑字仲承，河閒樂成人也。

translations.literal: Liu Shu, courtesy name Zhongcheng, was a man of Yuecheng in Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Shu, style Zhongcheng, came from Yuecheng in Hejian.

sentences.zh: 祖父稱，司隸校尉。

translations.literal: His grandfather Cheng was metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Liu Cheng had served as metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 淑少學明五經，遂隱居，立精舍講授，諸生常數百人。

translations.literal: In youth Shu studied and clarified the Five Classics; thereupon he dwelt in reclusion, established a refined hall for lecturing, and students were always several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the Five Classics in youth, then withdrew to a private academy that drew hundreds of students.

sentences.zh: 州郡禮請，五府連辟，並不就。

translations.literal: The commandery and province courteously summoned him; the five offices repeatedly summoned him—he did not go to any.
translations.idiomatic: Local authorities and all five high bureaus courted him with posts; he refused every call.

sentences.zh: 永興二年，司徒種暠舉淑賢良方正，辭以疾。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongxing, Minister of Education Chong Hao recommended Shu worthy and good, upright and correct; he declined citing illness.
translations.idiomatic: In 154 Minister Chong Hao nominated him worthy and upright; Liu Shu pleaded illness.

sentences.zh: 桓帝聞淑高名，切責州郡，使輿病詣京師。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan heard of Shu's lofty name, sternly reproached the province and commandery, and had him carried in illness to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan, hearing his fame, rebuked local officials until they carted the ailing scholar to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 淑不得已而赴洛陽，對策為天下第一，拜議郎。

translations.literal: Shu, having no choice, went to Luoyang; his palace examination answers were first under heaven; he was appointed gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to refuse further, he traveled to the capital, topped the examination, and received a consultant's rank.

sentences.zh: 又陳時政得失，災異之占，事皆效驗。

translations.literal: He also presented the gains and losses of current government and divinations of calamities and prodigies; in every case the affairs proved true in verification.
translations.idiomatic: He also critiqued policy and read omens from heaven; time after time his forecasts came true.

sentences.zh: 再遷尚書，納忠建議，多所補益。

translations.literal: Twice promoted to minister secretary, he brought in loyal counsel and set forth proposals, with much supplement and benefit.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted twice to the secretariat, he offered loyal advice that measurably improved governance.

sentences.zh: 又再遷侍中、虎賁中郎將。

translations.literal: Again twice promoted to palace attendant and colonel of the Rapid as Tigers.
translations.idiomatic: He rose twice more to palace attendant and colonel of the Tiger Ben guard.

sentences.zh: 上疏以為宜罷宦官，辭甚切直，帝雖不能用，亦不罪焉。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial holding that the eunuchs ought to be abolished; the wording was very cutting and straight; though the emperor could not employ it, neither did he punish him.
translations.idiomatic: He urged the abolition of the eunuch establishment in blunt terms; the emperor ignored the plea but did not punish him.

sentences.zh: 以淑宗室之賢，特加敬異，每有疑事，常密諮問之。

translations.literal: Because Shu was worthy of the imperial house, he was specially treated with respect and difference; whenever there were doubtful matters, he was always secretly consulted.
translations.idiomatic: As a respected member of the imperial clan, Liu Shu was often summoned for confidential counsel on doubtful points.

sentences.zh: 靈帝既位，宦官譖淑與竇武等通謀，下獄自殺。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ling had taken the throne, the eunuchs slandered Shu as secretly plotting with Dou Wu and others; he was cast into prison and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Ling had taken the throne, eunuchs accused him of conspiring with Dou Wu; he was jailed and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 李膺字符禮，穎川襄城人也。

translations.literal: Li Ying, courtesy name Yuanli, was a man of Xiangcheng in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying, known as Yuanli, came from Xiangcheng in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 祖父修，安帝時為太尉。

translations.literal: His grandfather Xiu, in Emperor An's time, was grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Li Xiu had been grand commandant under Emperor An.

sentences.zh: [一]父益，趙國相。

translations.literal: [1] His father Yi was chancellor of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His father Li Yi served as chancellor of the kingdom of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 膺性簡亢，無所交接，[二]唯以同郡荀淑、陳寔為師友。

translations.literal: Ying's nature was curt and lofty; he had no social dealings, [2] taking only Xun Shu and Chen Shi of the same commandery as teacher and friend.
translations.idiomatic: He was aloof and fastidious, [2] counting only Xun Shu and Chen Shi of Yingchuan as intimates.

sentences.zh: 注[一]漢官儀曰：「修字伯游。」

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Han guan yi says, "Xiu's courtesy name was Boyou."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Han guan yi gives Li Xiu the style Boyou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]亢，高也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: kang means high.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: kang here means proud or unbending.

sentences.zh: 初舉孝廉，為司徒胡廣所辟，舉高第，再遷青州刺史。

translations.literal: At first recommended filial and incorrupt, he was summoned by Minister of Education Hu Guang, advanced in high rank, twice promoted to inspector of Qing province.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and incorrupt, he entered Hu Guang's service, won top placement, and twice rose to inspector of Qingzhou.

sentences.zh: 守令畏威明，多望風□官。

translations.literal: Guardians and magistrates feared his awesome clarity; many, catching the wind, vacated office.
translations.idiomatic: Local magistrates dreaded his scrutiny; many resigned at rumor of his approach.

sentences.zh: 復征，再遷漁陽太守。

translations.literal: Again summoned, twice promoted to governor of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled to office, he advanced twice to governor of Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 尋轉蜀郡太守，以母老乞不之官。

translations.literal: Soon transferred to governor of Shu commandery; citing his mother's age he begged not to take up the post.
translations.idiomatic: He was ordered to Shu but pleaded out on grounds of his mother's age.

sentences.zh: [一]轉護烏桓校尉。

translations.literal: [1] He was transferred to colonel of the Wuhuan.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He was then named colonel protecting the Wuhuan.

sentences.zh: 鮮卑數犯塞，膺常蒙矢石，每破走之，虜甚憚懾。

translations.literal: The Xianbei repeatedly violated the passes; Ying often braved arrows and stones, each time defeating and routing them; the barbarians greatly feared him.
translations.idiomatic: He repeatedly drove back Xianbei raiders at personal risk, until the tribes learned to dread him.

sentences.zh: [二]以公事免官，還居綸氏，教授常千人。

translations.literal: [2] Removed from office on public grounds, he returned to dwell at Lunshi, teaching—always a thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Dismissed for official reasons, he retired to Lunshi, where a thousand students gathered.

sentences.zh: [三]南陽樊陵求為門徒，膺謝不受。

translations.literal: [3] Fan Ling of Nanyang sought to become a disciple; Ying declined and would not accept.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Fan Ling of Nanyang begged enrollment; Li Ying refused him.

sentences.zh: 陵後以阿附宦官，致位太尉，為節*[志]*者所羞。

translations.literal: Ling afterward, by fawning on eunuchs, reached the post of grand commandant, and was shamed by men of integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ling later curried eunuch favor to become grand commandant, to the disgust of honest men.

sentences.zh: [四]荀爽嘗就謁膺，因為其御，既還，喜曰：「今日乃得御李君矣。

translations.literal: [4] Xun Shuang once came to visit Ying and therefore drove his carriage for him; when he returned, he joyfully said, "Today I have been able to drive for Lord Li.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Xun Shuang once drove Li Ying's carriage as a gesture of respect and exclaimed afterward, "Today I drove for Master Li."

sentences.zh: 」其見慕如此。

translations.literal: " Such was the admiration he received.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the awe in which he was held.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「出補蜀郡太守，修庠序，設條教，明法令，威恩並行。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Xie Cheng's book says, "He went out to supplement as governor of Shu, repaired schools, set regulations, clarified statutes and commands; awe and kindness went together.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xie Cheng records that as governor of Shu he built schools, published regulations, and balanced severity with kindness.

sentences.zh: 蜀之珍玩，不入於門。

translations.literal: Shu's rare treasures did not enter his gate.
translations.idiomatic: No luxury gifts from Shu crossed his threshold.

sentences.zh: 益州紀其政化，朝廷舉能理劇，轉烏桓校尉。」

translations.literal: Yi Province recorded his government and transformation; the court cited him as able to manage acute cases, and transferred him to colonel of the Wuhuan.
translations.idiomatic: His administration was celebrated in the west; the court, citing his gift for knotty cases, moved him to the Wuhuan colonelcy.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「膺常率步騎臨陣交戰，身被創夷，拭血進戰，遂破寇，斬首二千級。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Xie Cheng's book says, "Ying often led foot and horse to meet the enemy in battle; his body was wounded; wiping blood he advanced in battle, thereupon broke the bandits, beheading two thousand grades."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Xie Cheng describes him leading troops in person, stanching his wounds and killing two thousand enemies in one engagement.

sentences.zh: 注[三]綸氏，縣，屬穎川郡，故城今陽城縣也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Lunshi is a county belonging to Yingchuan commandery; its old walled city is in Yangcheng county today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Lunshi lay in Yingchuan, near modern Yangcheng.

sentences.zh: 注[四]漢官儀曰：「樊陵字德雲。」

translations.literal: Comment [4]: the Han guan yi says, "Fan Ling's courtesy name was Deyun."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: the Han guan yi gives Fan Ling the style Deyun.

sentences.zh: 永壽二年，鮮卑寇雲中，桓帝聞膺能，乃復征為度遼將軍。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongshou, the Xianbei raided Yunzhong; Emperor Huan heard of Ying's ability and again summoned him as general who crosses the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: In 156 the Xianbei struck Yunzhong; Emperor Huan recalled Li Ying as general of the distant Liao frontier.

sentences.zh: 先是羌虜及疏勒、龜茲，數出攻鈔張掖、酒泉、雲中諸郡，百姓屢被其害。

translations.literal: Previously Qiang rebels and Shule and Qiuci had repeatedly gone out to attack and plunder Zhangye, Jiuquan, Yunzhong, and the various commanderies; the common people had repeatedly suffered their harm.
translations.idiomatic: Qiang and Tarim states had long raided the Hexi corridor, again and again harrying the border counties.

sentences.zh: 自膺到邊，皆望風懼服，先所掠男女，悉送還塞下。

translations.literal: From the time Ying reached the frontier, all gazed on the wind in fear and submission; men and women previously carried off were all sent back below the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Once Li Ying arrived, the raiders submitted without a fight and returned every captive taken in earlier seasons.

sentences.zh: 自是之後，聲振遠域。

translations.literal: From this afterward, his fame shook distant regions.
translations.idiomatic: His name thereafter carried terror and respect far beyond the wall.

sentences.zh: 延熹二年征，再遷河南尹。

translations.literal: Summoned in the second year of Yanxi, twice promoted to intendant of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: In 159 he was recalled and twice promoted to governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 時宛陵大姓羊元腢罷北海郡，臧罪狼藉，郡捨溷軒有奇巧，乃載之以歸。

translations.literal: At that time Yang Yuanou, a great clan of Wanling, had been dismissed from Beihai commandery; his corruption crimes were in disorder; the privy carriage in the commandery lodging had curious craft, and he loaded it to take home.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Yuanou, a magnate of Wanling, left a Beihai post under a cloud of corruption yet hauled off an elaborate privy from the official hostel.

sentences.zh: [一]膺表欲按其罪，元腢行賂宦豎，膺反坐輸作左校。

translations.literal: [1] Ying submitted a memorial wishing to investigate his crime; Yuanou practiced bribery on the eunuchs; Ying on the contrary suffered judgment and was sentenced to labor at the Left Work site.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Li Ying moved to indict him; Yang bought off the eunuchs, and Li Ying himself was sentenced to hard labor at the Left Work yards.

sentences.zh: 注[一]溷軒，廁屋。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: hun xuan is a privy building.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the hun xuan was a latrine annex of the hostel.

sentences.zh: 初，膺與廷尉馮緄、大司農劉祐等共同心志，糾罰奸幸，緄、祐時亦得罪輸作。

translations.literal: At first Ying, with Commandant of Justice Feng Gun, Minister of Finance Liu You, and others, shared one mind, investigating and punishing the wicked and favored; Gun and You at the time also suffered guilt and were sentenced to labor.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying had joined Feng Gun and Liu You in prosecuting favorites until all three were railroaded into the labor gangs.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉應奉上疏理膺等曰：「昔秦人觀寶於楚，昭奚恤馬以腢賢；

translations.literal: Metropolitan Commandant Ying Feng submitted a memorial arguing the case for Ying and others, saying, "Formerly the people of Qin went to Chu to view its treasures; Zhao Xixu answered with horses to show worthiness;
translations.idiomatic: Metropolitan Commandant Ying Feng petitioned for Li Ying, opening with the tale of Qin envoys who came to size up Chu.

sentences.zh: [一]梁惠王瑋其照乘之珠，齊威王荅以四臣。

translations.literal: [1] King Hui of Wei prized his pearl that lights the carriage; King Wei of Qi answered with four ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He compared King Hui of Wei boasting of his luminous pearl to King Wei of Qi naming four ministers as his true jewels.

sentences.zh: [二]夫忠賢武將，國之心膂。

translations.literal: [2] Loyal worthies and martial generals are the heart and liver of the state.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Loyal ministers and able generals are the sinews of the realm.

sentences.zh: 竊見左校□刑徒前廷尉馮緄、大司農劉祐、河南尹李膺等，執法不撓，誅舉邪臣，肆之以法，[三]觿庶稱宜。

translations.literal: [3] I have observed the convict laborers at the Left Work site, former Commandant of Justice Feng Gun, Minister of Finance Liu You, Intendant of Henan Li Ying, and others: in holding the law they did not bend; in executing and reporting wicked ministers they displayed them according to law; the multitude deemed it fitting.
translations.idiomatic: [3] He named Feng Gun, Liu You, and Li Ying among the convicts, praising their unbending enforcement of the law against corrupt power.

sentences.zh: 昔季孫行父親逆君命，逐出莒僕，於舜之功二十之一。

translations.literal: Formerly Jisun Hangfu in person opposed the ruler's command, drove out the steward of Ju, and for Shun's merit it was one in twenty.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Jisun Hangfu expelling the murderous steward of Ju as one-twentieth of Shun's twenty great deeds.

sentences.zh: [四]今膺等投身強禦，畢力致罪，陛下既不聽察，而猥受譖訴，遂令忠臣同愆元惡。

translations.literal: [4] Now Ying and others threw themselves against the powerful, exhausted strength and incurred guilt; Your Majesty has neither listened nor examined, but rashly accepted slanderous complaints, thereby causing loyal ministers to share guilt with arch-villains.
translations.idiomatic: [4] He protested that the emperor had swallowed slander and lumped loyal officials with real criminals.

sentences.zh: 自春迄冬，不蒙降恕，遐邇觀聽，為之歎息。

translations.literal: From spring through winter they have not received lowering of punishment or forgiveness; far and near, observers and listeners sigh for them.
translations.idiomatic: They languished from spring to winter while the empire looked on in dismay.

sentences.zh: 夫立政之要，記功忘失，是以武帝捨安國於徒中，[五]宣帝征張敞於亡命。

translations.literal: The essential of establishing government is to record merit and forget fault; therefore Emperor Wu took Han Anguo from among convicts, [5] Emperor Xuan summoned Zhang Chang from flight as a fugitive.
translations.idiomatic: Good government remembers service and overlooks slips, as Han Wudi freed Han Anguo and Xuandi recalled Zhang Chang from exile.

sentences.zh: [六]緄前討蠻荊，均吉甫之功。

translations.literal: [6] Gun formerly campaigned against the Man of Jing, matching the merit of Jifu.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Feng Gun's southern campaign matched the ode's praise of Fang Shu.

sentences.zh: [七] 祐數臨督司，有不吐茹之節。

translations.literal: [7] You repeatedly attended the directorate of retainers, with the integrity of neither swallowing soft nor spitting hard.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Liu You had repeatedly served as metropolitan commandant with the steadfast courage praised in the Odes.

sentences.zh: [八]膺著威幽、並，遺愛度遼。

translations.literal: [8] Ying displayed awe in You and Bing; he left kindness at the crossing of the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: [8] Li Ying's prestige had secured Youzhou and Bingzhou and his kindness still lingered on the Liao frontier.

sentences.zh: 今三垂蠢動，王旅未振。

translations.literal: Now the three margins stir like worms; the royal host has not yet been roused.
translations.idiomatic: The borders were already restless while imperial armies stood idle.

sentences.zh: 易稱『雷雨作解，君子以赦過宥罪』。

translations.literal: The Changes says, "Thunder and rain act and release; the noble man pardons faults and forgives crimes."
translations.idiomatic: He quoted the Image of the hexagram Jie: the gentleman pardons error and remits punishment.

sentences.zh: [九]乞原膺等，以備不虞。

translations.literal: [9] I beg to pardon Ying and others in order to prepare against the unforeseen.
translations.idiomatic: [9] He begged a general pardon to meet emergencies on the frontier.

sentences.zh: 」書奏，乃悉免其刑。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, thereupon all their punishments were remitted.
translations.idiomatic: The throne accepted the plea and freed them all.

sentences.zh: 注[一]新序曰：「秦欲伐楚，使*[使]*者往觀楚之寶器。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Xin xu says, "Qin wished to attack Chu and sent an envoy to observe Chu's precious vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Xin xu story of Qin scouting Chu's treasures opens the commentary.

sentences.zh: 楚王聞之，召昭奚恤問焉。

translations.literal: The king of Chu heard it and summoned Zhao Xixu to ask.
