sentences.zh: 後牢修誣言鉤黨，[一]滂坐系黃門北寺獄。

translations.literal: Later Lao Xiu slandered hooked partisans; [1] Pang sat in bonds in the Yellow Gate North Prison.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Lao Xiu's memorial landed Fan Pang in the eunuch prison.

sentences.zh: 獄吏謂曰：「凡坐系皆祭□陶。

translations.literal: The jailer said to him, "All who sit in bonds should sacrifice to Gao Yao."
translations.idiomatic: The turnkey told him to worship the spirit of justice.

sentences.zh: 」滂曰：「□陶賢者，古之直臣。

translations.literal: Pang said, "Gao Yao was a worthy, a straight minister of antiquity;
translations.idiomatic: Fan Pang called Gao Yao a model judge.

sentences.zh: 知滂無罪，將理之於帝；

translations.literal: knowing Pang was guiltless he would plead for him to the High Lord;
translations.idiomatic: Such a spirit would plead his innocence to heaven.

sentences.zh: [二]如其有罪，祭之何益！」

translations.literal: [2] if I were guilty, what use would sacrifice be?
translations.idiomatic: [2] If he were guilty, prayer would be mockery.

sentences.zh: 觿人由此亦止。

translations.literal: The multitude thereupon also stopped.
translations.idiomatic: Other prisoners dropped the idea too.

sentences.zh: 獄吏將加掠考，滂以同囚多嬰病，乃請先就格，遂與同郡袁忠爭受楚毒。

translations.literal: When the jailer was about to apply torture, Pang because many fellow prisoners were ill asked to take the rack first, and contended with Yuan Zhong of the same commandery to receive the Chu torture.
translations.idiomatic: He volunteered for torture first to spare sick cellmates, rivaling Yuan Zhong for the rack.

sentences.zh: 桓帝使中常侍王甫以次辨詰，滂等皆三木囊頭，暴於階下。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan sent Regular Attendant Wang Fu to question them in order; Pang and the others all wore the three woods with heads in sacks, exposed below the steps.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu interrogated them in fetters and hoods on the palace steps.

sentences.zh: [三]餘人在前，或對或否，滂﹑忠於後越次而進。

translations.literal: [3] The others before him answered or not; Pang and Zhong from behind skipped their turn and advanced.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Fan Pang and Yuan Zhong pushed forward out of turn.

sentences.zh: 王甫詰曰：「君為人臣，不惟忠國，而共造部黨，自相□舉，評論朝廷，虛構無端，諸所謀結，並欲何為？

translations.literal: Wang Fu questioned, saying, "You as ministers did not think loyally of the state yet jointly formed a faction, recommending one another, criticizing the court, inventing baseless slanders—what did all your plotting intend?"
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu demanded they confess to forming an anti-court cabal.

sentences.zh: 皆以情對，不得隱飾。

translations.literal: "Answer according to the facts without concealing or glossing."
translations.idiomatic: He ordered plain truth, no evasions.

sentences.zh: 」滂對曰：「臣聞仲尼之言，『見善如不及，見惡如探湯』。

translations.literal: " Pang replied, "Your servant has heard Zhongni's saying: see good as if unable to catch up, see evil as if dipping the hand in boiling water.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Pang quoted Confucius on zeal for good and horror of evil.

sentences.zh: [四] 欲使善善同其清，惡惡同其污，謂王政之所願聞，不悟更以為黨。

translations.literal: [4] I wished to make the good share their cleanness and the evil share their filth, thinking that is what kingly government wishes to hear—I did not realize it was instead called faction.
translations.idiomatic: [4] He said he had only tried to align the court with virtue.

sentences.zh: 」甫曰：「卿更相拔舉，迭為唇齒，有不合者，見則排斥，其意如何？

translations.literal: " Fu said, "You mutually promote each other, alternately acting as lips and teeth; whoever disagrees you reject—what is your intent?
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu pressed him on mutual recommendation.

sentences.zh: 」滂乃慷慨仰天曰：「古之循善，自求多福；

translations.literal: " Pang then, impassioned, looked up at heaven and said, "In antiquity pursuing good brought many blessings;
translations.idiomatic: Fan Pang cried that ancient virtue won reward.

sentences.zh: 今之循善，身陷大戮。

translations.literal: now pursuing good brings the body into great execution.
translations.idiomatic: Today virtue earns the executioner.

sentences.zh: 身死之日，願埋滂於首陽山側，上不負皇天，下不愧夷﹑齊。」

translations.literal: On the day I die I wish to be buried beside Shouyang Mountain—above not shaming bright heaven, below not shaming Yi and Qi.
translations.idiomatic: He asked to lie near Boyi and Shuqi's grave on Shouyang.

sentences.zh: [五]甫愍然為之改容。

translations.literal: [5] Fu compassionally changed expression for him.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Even Wang Fu was moved.

sentences.zh: 乃得並解桎梏。

translations.literal: Thereupon they were all released from fetters and cangue.
translations.idiomatic: Their shackles were struck off.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]鉤，引也。

translations.literal: [6] Comment [1]: gou means to draw out or hook.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: gou in gou dang means to implicate.

sentences.zh: 注[二]帝謂天也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: di means heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: High Lord here means heaven or the throne.

sentences.zh: 注[三]三木，項及手足皆有械，更以物蒙覆其頭也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: the three woods means fetters on neck, hands, and feet; moreover something covers the head.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: three-wood fetters bound neck, wrists, and ankles, with a hood over the head.

sentences.zh: 前書司馬遷曰「魏其，大將也，衣赭關三木」也。

translations.literal: The former book's Sima Qian says, "The Marquis of Wei was a great general, yet wore cinnabar and the three woods."
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu compares the humiliation to Wei Qi in fetters.

sentences.zh: 注[四]探湯喻去疾也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: dipping the hand in boiling soup is a metaphor for hasting to remove evil.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: the boiling cauldron image means instant revulsion at evil.

sentences.zh: 見論語。

translations.literal: See the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: The quotation is from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[五]伯夷﹑叔齊餓死首陽山，見史記。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: Boyi and Shuqi starved on Shouyang Mountain—see the Records of the Historian.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: the Shiji tells of Boyi and Shuqi on Shouyang.

sentences.zh: 首陽山在洛陽東北。

translations.literal: Shouyang Mountain lies northeast of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Shouyang lies northeast of Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 注[六]鄭玄注周禮曰：「木在足曰桎，在手曰梏。」

translations.literal: Comment [6]: Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Zhou li says, "Wood on the feet is called zhi; on the hands is called gu."
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: Zheng Xuan distinguishes ankle stocks from hand stocks.

sentences.zh: 滂後事釋，南歸。

translations.literal: After Pang's affair was cleared he returned south.
translations.idiomatic: When he was cleared he headed home south.

sentences.zh: 始發京師，汝南﹑南陽士大夫迎之者數千兩。

translations.literal: When he first set out from the capital, several thousand carriages of gentlemen from Runan and Nanyang welcomed him.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of carriages of Runan and Nanyang gentry met him leaving Luoyang.

sentences.zh: [一]同囚鄉人殷陶﹑黃穆，亦免俱歸，並□侍於滂，應對賓客。

translations.literal: [1] Fellow prisoners Yin Tao and Huang Mu of his township were also released and returned together; they attended on Pang and answered guests.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Two fellow townsfolk who shared his cell insisted on escorting him.

sentences.zh: 滂顧謂陶等曰：「今子相隨，是重吾禍也。

translations.literal: Pang turned and said to Tao and the others, "Your following me now doubles my disaster."
translations.idiomatic: He told them their company would only deepen suspicion.

sentences.zh: 」遂遁還鄉里。

translations.literal: " Thereupon they slipped away and returned to their hamlets.
translations.idiomatic: They slipped home separately.

sentences.zh: 注[一]兩，車也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: liang means carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: liang here counts carriages, not pairs of horses only.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰：「戎車三百兩。」

translations.literal: The Documents says, "War chariots three hundred liang."
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Documents counts war carts by liang.

sentences.zh: 初，滂等系獄，尚書霍諝理之。

translations.literal: At first, when Pang and others were bound in prison, Minister Secretary Huo Xu argued their case.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Pang was jailed, Huo Xu pleaded for him at the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 及得免，到京師，往候諝而不為謝。

translations.literal: When they obtained release and reached the capital, he went to visit Xu but did not offer thanks.
translations.idiomatic: After his release Fan Pang called on Huo Xu without a word of gratitude.

sentences.zh: 或有讓滂者。

translations.literal: Some reproached Pang.
translations.idiomatic: Some criticized him for ingratitude.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「昔叔向嬰罪，祁奚救之，未聞羊舌有謝恩之辭，祁老有自伐之色。」

translations.literal: He replied, "Formerly Shu Xiang suffered guilt and Xi Qi saved him; I have not heard that Yangshe offered words of thanks for grace, nor that the old Xi wore a look of self-praise."
translations.idiomatic: He cited the Zuo story: Shu Xiang never thanked Xi Qi aloud, nor did Xi Qi boast of the rescue.

sentences.zh: 竟無所言。

translations.literal: In the end he said nothing.
translations.idiomatic: He let the point stand without another word.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]左傳，晉討欒盈之黨，殺叔向之弟羊舌虎，並囚叔向。

translations.literal: [1] Comment [1]: the Zuo Commentary—Jin punished Luan Ying's faction, killed Shu Xiang's younger brother Yangshe Hu, and imprisoned Shu Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note [1]: the gloss retells Jin's purge of Luan Ying and Shu Xiang's arrest.

sentences.zh: 於是祁奚聞之，見范宣子曰：「夫謀而鮮過，惠訓不倦者，叔向有焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon Xi Qi heard it and saw Fan Xuanzi, saying, "He who plans yet seldom errs, who instructs with kindness and does not weary—Shu Xiang has these qualities.
translations.idiomatic: Xi Qi told Fan Xuanzi that Shu Xiang was the state's pillar.

sentences.zh: 社稷之固也，猶將十代宥之，今一不免其身，不亦惑乎？

translations.literal: He is the bulwark of the altars of soil and grain; still he would be pardoned for ten generations—now once he cannot save his own person—is it not perplexing?
translations.idiomatic: He argued that such a man deserved amnesty, not execution.

sentences.zh: 」宣子說而免之。

translations.literal: " Xuanzi was pleased and released him.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Xuanzi agreed and freed Shu Xiang.

sentences.zh: 祁奚不見叔向而歸，叔向亦不告免焉而朝。

translations.literal: Xi Qi did not see Shu Xiang but returned; Shu Xiang likewise did not announce his release but went to court.
translations.idiomatic: Neither man made a show of the rescue—they simply resumed duty.

sentences.zh: 孔安國注尚書曰「自功曰伐」也。

translations.literal: Kong Anguo's commentary on the Documents says, "To praise one's own merit is called fa."
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo defines fa as boasting of one's own deeds.

sentences.zh: 建寧二年，遂大誅黨人，詔下急捕滂等。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianning thereupon came the great slaughter of partisans; an edict went down urgently to arrest Pang and others.
translations.idiomatic: In 169 the second partisan purge ordered Fan Pang's arrest.

sentences.zh: 督郵吳導至縣，抱詔書，閉傳捨，伏默而泣。

translations.literal: Supervising secretary Wu Dao reached the county, hugged the edict document, closed the relay hostel, and wept in silence.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Dao locked himself in the post station with the warrant and wept.

sentences.zh: [一]滂聞之，曰：「必為我也。

translations.literal: [1] Pang heard it and said, "It must be on my account."
translations.idiomatic: [1] Fan Pang knew the warrant was for him.

sentences.zh: 」即自詣獄。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he went to the jail himself.
translations.idiomatic: He walked to prison and surrendered.

sentences.zh: 縣令郭揖大驚，出解印綬，引與俱亡。

translations.literal: Magistrate Guo Yi was greatly startled, came out, removed his seal and cord, and led him to flee together.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Guo Yi tried to resign and flee with him.

sentences.zh: 曰：「天下大矣，子何為在此？

translations.literal: He said, "The world is vast—why should you stay here?"
translations.idiomatic: He urged Fan Pang to run while he could.

sentences.zh: 」滂曰：「滂死則禍塞，何敢以罪累君，又令老母流離乎！

translations.literal: " Pang said, "If Pang dies then the disaster is stopped—how dare I burden you with my crime and also make my aged mother wander in exile?
translations.idiomatic: Fan Pang refused to drag the magistrate or his mother into his fate.

sentences.zh: 」其母就與之訣。

translations.literal: " His mother came to take leave of him.
translations.idiomatic: His mother came for a final farewell.

sentences.zh: 滂白母曰：「仲博孝敬，足以供養，[二] 滂從龍舒君歸黃泉，[三]存亡各得其所。

translations.literal: Pang told his mother, "Zhongbo is filial and respectful—enough to provide support; [2] Pang will follow Lord Longshu to the yellow springs; [3] the living and dead each obtain their place.
translations.idiomatic: [2-3] He told her his brother could support her while he joined his dead father.

sentences.zh: 惟大人割不可忍之恩，勿增感戚。」

translations.literal: Only, my elder, cut the love that cannot be borne—do not add to grief.
translations.idiomatic: He begged her to choke back grief for his sake.

sentences.zh: 母曰：「汝今得與李﹑杜齊名，死亦何恨！

translations.literal: The mother said, "You now attain standing with Li and Du in name—what regret in death?"
translations.idiomatic: She said to die beside Li Ying and Du Mi was honor enough.

sentences.zh: [四]既有令名，復求壽考，可兼得乎？」

translations.literal: [4] Having already a fair name, yet also seeking long life—can you have both?
translations.idiomatic: [4] She asked whether fame and long life could both be his.

sentences.zh: 滂跪受教，再拜而辭。

translations.literal: Pang knelt, received her teaching, bowed twice, and took leave.
translations.idiomatic: He knelt, bowed twice, and went to his death.

sentences.zh: 顧謂其子曰：「吾欲使汝為惡，則惡不可為；

translations.literal: Turning to his son he said, "I wish to make you do evil—yet evil cannot be done;
translations.idiomatic: He told his son evil was not a path he could teach.

sentences.zh: 使汝為善，則我不為惡。

translations.literal: if I make you do good, then I have not done evil.
translations.idiomatic: His own death for good, he said, was the lesson for his son.

sentences.zh: 」行路聞之，莫不流涕。

translations.literal: " Wayfarers who heard it none did not shed tears.
translations.idiomatic: Every bystander wept.

sentences.zh: 時年三十三。

translations.literal: At the time he was thirty-three years old.
translations.idiomatic: He was thirty-three.

sentences.zh: 注[一]傳，驛捨也，音知戀反。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: chuan is a relay hostel; the reading is the zhi-lian fan cut.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: chuan means the post-house; the commentary gives its fanqie.

sentences.zh: 注[二]仲博，滂弟也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Zhongbo is Pang's younger brother.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Zhongbo is Fan Pang's brother.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「滂父顯，故龍舒侯相也。」

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Xie Cheng's book says, "Pang's father Xian was former chancellor to the marquis of Longshu."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Fan Xian had been chancellor to the marquis of Longshu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]李膺﹑杜密。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: Li Ying and Du Mi.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Li and Du are Li Ying and Du Mi.

sentences.zh: 論曰：李膺振拔污險之中，[一]蘊義生風，以鼓動流俗，[二]激素行以恥威權，立廉尚以振貴埶，使天下之士奮迅感□，波蕩而從之，幽深牢破室族而不顧，至於子伏其死而母歡其義。

translations.literal: The discourse says: Li Ying pulled himself up from amid mire and peril, [1] storing righteousness until it became a wind to stir the vulgar, [2] heightening plain conduct to shame awesome power, setting integrity high to shake noble influence, so that gentlemen under heaven rose in swift response, surging in waves to follow him—breaking hidden prisons, shattering clans, yet not looking back, until sons lay down their lives and mothers rejoiced in the right.
translations.idiomatic: The historian praises Li Ying for rousing the elite against corruption until men died gladly and mothers approved.

sentences.zh: 壯矣哉！

translations.literal: Magnificent indeed!
translations.idiomatic: What grandeur!

sentences.zh: 子曰：「道之將廢也與？

translations.literal: The Master said, "When the Way is about to be cast aside—is it not fate?"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius asked whether the Way's eclipse was fate.

sentences.zh: 命也！

translations.literal: It is mandate!
translations.idiomatic: He called it Heaven's decree.

sentences.zh: 」[三] 注[一]前書班固曰「振拔污塗，跨騰風雲」也。

translations.literal: " [3] Comment [1]: the former book's Ban Gu says, "He pulled himself from the muddy road and leaped through wind and cloud.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note [1]: Ban Gu's phrase on rising from the mire.

sentences.zh: 注[二]周易曰：「鼓以動之。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the Zhou Changes says, "The drum is used to move them."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: the Changes uses the drum as image of stirring action.

sentences.zh: 注[三]論語之文。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: text from the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: the closing quote is from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 尹勳字伯元，河南鞏人也。

translations.literal: Yin Xun, courtesy name Boyuan, was a man of Gong in Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Xun, style Boyuan, came from Gong in Henan.

sentences.zh: 家世衣冠。

translations.literal: For generations his family wore caps and sashes.
translations.idiomatic: His house had long held high office.

sentences.zh: 伯父睦為司徒，兄頌為太尉，宗族多居貴位者，而勳獨持清操，不以地埶尚人。

translations.literal: His uncle Mu was minister of education, his elder brother Song grand commandant; many of the lineage held noble rank, yet Xun alone kept pure conduct and did not lord his status over others.
translations.idiomatic: Though uncles and brothers were grandees, Yin Xun stayed modest.

sentences.zh: 州郡連辟，察孝廉，三遷邯鄲令，政有異多。

translations.literal: The commandery repeatedly summoned him; examined filial and incorrupt; thrice promoted magistrate of Handan—his government had unusual merit.
translations.idiomatic: He rose through Handan with a singular reputation for governance.

sentences.zh: 後舉高第，五遷尚書令。

translations.literal: Later raised in high rank, five times promoted to minister secretary.
translations.idiomatic: He climbed five steps to minister secretary.

sentences.zh: 及桓帝誅大將軍梁冀，勳參建大謀，封都鄉侯。

translations.literal: When Emperor Huan executed Great General Liang Ji, Xun took part in framing the great plan and was enfeoffed village marquis of Dou.
translations.idiomatic: He helped plan Liang Ji's fall and won a village marquisate.

sentences.zh: 遷汝南太守。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: He became governor of Runan.

sentences.zh: 上書解釋范滂﹑袁忠等黨議禁錮。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial explaining Fan Pang, Yuan Zhong, and others caught in partisan proscription.
translations.idiomatic: He petitioned to clear Fan Pang and Yuan Zhong of the ban.

sentences.zh: 尋征拜將作大匠，轉大司農。

translations.literal: Soon summoned minister of works, transferred minister of finance.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled minister of works, then minister of finance.

sentences.zh: 坐竇武等事，下獄自殺。

translations.literal: Sitting on the affair of Dou Wu and others, he was cast into prison and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Wu's coup trapped him in prison; he took his own life.

sentences.zh: 蔡衍字孟喜，汝南項人也。

translations.literal: Cai Yan, courtesy name Mengxi, was a man of Xiang in Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yan, style Mengxi, came from Xiang in Runan.

sentences.zh: [一]少明經講授，以禮讓化鄉里。

translations.literal: [1] In youth he clarified the classics in lecturing and transformed his village by ritual yielding.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He taught the classics and taught his neighbors courtesy.

sentences.zh: 鄉里有爭訟者，輒詣衍決之，其所平處，皆曰無怨。

translations.literal: When the village had quarrels and lawsuits they always went to Yan to decide them; wherever he balanced cases people said they had no resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Villagers brought every dispute to him and left satisfied.

sentences.zh: 注[一]項，今陳州項城縣也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Xiang is present Xiangcheng county in Chenzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xiang is modern Xiangcheng in Henan.

sentences.zh: 羊陟字嗣祖，太山梁父人也。

translations.literal: Yang Zhi, courtesy name Sizu, was a man of Liangfu in Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Zhi, style Sizu, came from Liangfu in Taishan.

sentences.zh: [一]家世冠族。

translations.literal: [1] For generations his family was a great clan of cap and sash.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His clan ranked among the great houses.

sentences.zh: 陟少清直有學行，舉孝廉，辟太尉李固府，舉高第，拜侍御史。

translations.literal: Zhi in youth was pure, straight, and had learning and conduct; recommended filial and incorrupt, summoned to Grand Commandant Li Gu's office, raised in high rank, appointed attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and incorrupt, he served Li Gu and became attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 會固被誅，陟以故吏禁錮歷年。

translations.literal: When Gu was executed, Zhi as a former subordinate was proscribed for years.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu's death barred him from office for years.

sentences.zh: 復舉高第，再遷冀州刺史。

translations.literal: Again raised in high rank, twice promoted inspector of Ji Province.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled, he rose twice to inspector of Ji Province.

sentences.zh: 奏案貪濁，所在肅然。

translations.literal: He memorialized and investigated greed and filth; wherever he went there was awe.
translations.idiomatic: His investigations terrified corrupt officials.

sentences.zh: 又再遷虎賁中郎將﹑城門校尉，三遷尚書令。

translations.literal: Again twice promoted colonel of the Rapid as Tigers and colonel of the city gates; thrice promoted minister secretary.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to Tiger Ben colonel, city-gates colonel, then minister secretary.

sentences.zh: 時太尉張顥﹑司徒樊陵﹑大鴻臚郭防﹑太僕曹陵﹑大司農馮方並與宦豎相姻私，公行貨賂，並奏罷黜之，不納。

translations.literal: At the time Grand Commandant Zhang Hao, Minister of Education Fan Ling, grand herald Guo Fang, grand coachman Cao Ling, and Minister of Finance Feng Fang all had private marriage ties with eunuchs and openly took bribes; together he memorialized to dismiss them, but it was not accepted.
translations.idiomatic: He impeached a ring of ministers tied to eunuchs; the throne ignored it.

sentences.zh: 以前太尉劉寵﹑司隸校尉許冰﹑幽州刺史楊熙﹑涼州刺史劉恭﹑益州刺史龐艾清亮在公，薦舉升進。

translations.literal: He recommended former grand commandant Liu Chong, Metropolitan Commandant Xu Bing, Inspector of You Yang Xi, Inspector of Liang Liu Gong, and Inspector of Yi Pang Ai as pure, bright, and fair to the public for promotion.
translations.idiomatic: He nominated honest men such as Liu Chong and Xu Bing for higher rank.

sentences.zh: 帝嘉之，拜陟河南尹。

translations.literal: The emperor praised him and appointed Zhi intendant of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor made him governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 計日受奉，常食干飯茹菜，禁制豪右，京師憚之。

translations.literal: He counted days to receive salary, always ate dried rice and vegetables, restrained the powerful families of the right, and the capital feared him.
translations.idiomatic: He lived on dry rations and cracked down on Luoyang magnates.

sentences.zh: 會黨事起，免官禁錮，卒於家。

translations.literal: When the partisan affair arose he was dismissed and proscribed and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: The proscription ended his career; he died at home.

sentences.zh: 注[一]梁父故城在今兗州泗水縣北。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Liangfu's old walled city lies north of Sishui in Yanzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: old Liangfu lay north of Sishui, Shandong.

sentences.zh: 張儉字符節，山陽高平人，趙王張耳之後也。

translations.literal: Zhang Jian, courtesy name Yuanjie, was a man of Gaoping in Shanyang, a descendant of King Zhang Er of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jian, style Yuanjie, of Gaoping in Shanyang, traced descent from Zhao's Zhang Er.

sentences.zh: [一]父成，江夏太守。

translations.literal: [1] His father Cheng was governor of Jiangxia.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His father Zhang Cheng governed Jiangxia.

sentences.zh: 儉初舉茂才，以刺史非其人，謝病不起。

translations.literal: Jian at first was recommended flourishing talent; because the inspector was not the right man he declined illness and did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: He refused a flourishing-talent summons under a corrupt inspector.

sentences.zh: 注[一]張耳，大梁人也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Zhang Er was a man of Daliang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Zhang Er came from Daliang.

sentences.zh: 高祖立為趙王。

translations.literal: Gaozu established him as king of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu made him king of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 延熹八年，太守翟超請為東部督郵。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of Yanxi the governor Zhai Chao appointed him supervising secretary of the eastern division.
translations.idiomatic: In 165 Governor Zhai Chao named him eastern supervising secretary.

sentences.zh: 時中常侍侯覽家在防東，[一]殘暴百姓，所為不軌。

translations.literal: At the time Regular Attendant Hou Lan's family was east of Fang, [1] cruelly harming the common people and acting beyond the track.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Hou Lan's kin terrorized Fangdong.

sentences.zh: 儉舉劾覽及其母罪惡，請誅之。

translations.literal: Jian raised a memorial impeaching Lan and his mother's crimes and wickedness, asking to execute them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jian memorialized for Hou Lan's execution.

sentences.zh: 覽遏絕章表，並不得通，由是結仇。

translations.literal: Lan blocked and cut off memorials so none could get through; on that account enmity was knotted.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Lan suppressed the memorials and sealed their feud.

sentences.zh: 鄉人朱並，素性佞邪，為儉所□，並懷怨恚，遂上書告儉與同郡二十四人為黨，於是刊章討捕。

translations.literal: His townsman Zhu Bing, by nature fawning and crooked, had been [struck] by Jian; Bing harbored resentment and thereupon submitted a memorial reporting Jian and twenty-four men of the same commandery as a faction; thereupon the text was shaved and pursuit ordered.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Bing, whom Zhang Jian had shamed, denounced him as head of a twenty-five-man clique.

sentences.zh: 儉得亡命，困迫遁走，望門投止，莫不重其名行，破家兼容。

translations.literal: Jian obtained flight for his life, pressed hard and fleeing, knocking at doors for shelter—none did not weight his name and conduct; they broke their households to shelter him.
translations.idiomatic: Fugitives across the realm hid him at ruinous cost.

sentences.zh: 後流轉東萊，止李篤家。

translations.literal: Later he drifted to Donglai and stopped at Li Du's house.
translations.idiomatic: He reached Donglai and the home of Li Du.

sentences.zh: 外黃令毛欽操兵到門，篤引欽謂曰：「張儉知名天下，而亡非其罪。

translations.literal: Magistrate of Waihuang Mao Qin bearing arms reached the door; Du led Qin aside and said, "Zhang Jian is famed under heaven, yet his flight is not for his crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Mao Qin came with troops; Li Du argued Zhang Jian was innocent.

sentences.zh: 縱儉可得，寧忍執之乎？

translations.literal: Even if Jian could be caught, could you bear to seize him?
translations.idiomatic: He asked whether Mao Qin could arrest a man he knew was blameless.

sentences.zh: 」欽因起撫篤曰：「蘧伯玉恥獨為君子，足下如何自專仁義？

translations.literal: " Qin thereupon rose, stroked Du, and said, "Qu Boyu was ashamed to be the only gentleman; how can you alone monopolize benevolence and right?
translations.idiomatic: Mao Qin cited Qu Boyu's shame at solitary virtue.

sentences.zh: 」篤曰：「篤雖好義，明廷今日載其半矣。

translations.literal: " Du said, "Though Du loves right, my bright court today carries half of it.
translations.idiomatic: Li Du answered that the state already owned half the credit by not forcing the arrest.

sentences.zh: 」[二]欽歎息而去。

translations.literal: " [2] Qin sighed and departed.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Mao Qin sighed and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 篤因緣送儉出塞，以故得免。

translations.literal: Du by a connection sent Jian out beyond the passes, and so he escaped.
translations.idiomatic: Li Du smuggled him beyond the frontier to safety.

sentences.zh: 其所經歷，伏重誅者以十數，宗親並皆殄滅，郡縣為之殘破。

translations.literal: Where he passed, those who suffered heavy execution numbered in the tens; kin by clan were all exterminated; commanderies and counties were broken on his account.
translations.idiomatic: His flight cost dozens of hosts execution and shattered whole counties.

sentences.zh: 注[一]縣名，屬山陽郡，故城在今兗州金鄉縣南。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: it is a county name belonging to Shanyang commandery; the old walled city lies south of Jinxiang in Yanzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Fangdong county lay south of Jinxiang, Shandong.

sentences.zh: 注[二]明廷猶明府。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: ming ting is like ming fu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: ming ting means the same as ming fu, "your excellency."

sentences.zh: 言不執儉，得義之半也。

translations.literal: It means that by not seizing Jian he obtained half of the righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Letting Zhang Jian go counted as half the moral victory.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，黨事解，乃還鄉里。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping the partisan affair was lifted and he returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: In 184 the ban ended and he went home.

sentences.zh: 大將軍、三公並辟，又舉敦樸，公車特徵，起家拜少府，皆不就。

translations.literal: The great general and three dukes jointly summoned him; again he was recommended as honest and plain; the public carriage specially summoned him; he rose from common status to be appointed minister of the household—all he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: High office called him repeatedly; he refused every post.

sentences.zh: 獻帝初，百姓饑荒，而儉資計差溫，乃傾竭財產，與邑裡共之，賴其存者以百數。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Xian's reign the hundred surnames were starving in famine, yet Jian's resources were somewhat warm; thereupon he poured out his property and shared it with the village and hamlet; those who survived by it numbered in the hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: In the famine he fed hundreds from his own stores.

sentences.zh: 建安初，征為衛尉，不得已而起。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jian'an he was summoned captain of the guard and, having no choice, rose.
translations.idiomatic: In 196 he could no longer refuse the captaincy of the guard.

sentences.zh: 儉見曹氏世德已萌，乃闔門懸車，不豫政事。

translations.literal: Jian saw that the Cao family's generation of virtue had already sprouted; thereupon he barred his gate, hung up his carriage, and did not take part in government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Cao power rise, he sealed his gate and withdrew from politics.

sentences.zh: 歲余卒於許下。

translations.literal: After more than a year he died at Xu.
translations.idiomatic: He died at Xu a year later.

sentences.zh: 年八十四。

translations.literal: His years were eighty-four.
translations.idiomatic: He was eighty-four.

sentences.zh: 論曰：昔魏齊違死，虞卿解印；

translations.literal: The discourse says: Formerly Wei Qi fled death and Yu Qing surrendered his seal;
translations.idiomatic: The historian compares him to Wei Qi and Yu Qing.

sentences.zh: [一]季布逃亡，朱家甘罪。

translations.literal: [1] Ji Bu fled in exile and Zhu Jia willingly took guilt.
translations.idiomatic: [1] And to Ji Bu sheltered by Zhu Jia.

sentences.zh: [二]而張儉見怒時王，顛沛假命，天下聞其風者，莫不憐其壯志，而爭為之主。

translations.literal: [2] Yet Zhang Jian incurred the wrath of the king of the age, wandered in peril borrowing life—those who heard his wind under heaven pitied his high purpose and vied to be his host.
translations.idiomatic: [2] All the empire pitied Zhang Jian and competed to hide him.

sentences.zh: 至乃捐城委爵、破族屠身，蓋數十百所，豈不賢哉！

translations.literal: To the point of abandoning cities, casting aside noble rank, shattering clans and slaughtering bodies—perhaps tens or hundreds of places—were they not worthy?
translations.idiomatic: Hosts ruined themselves by the score—could such men be less than heroic?

sentences.zh: 然儉以區區一掌，而欲獨堙江河，[三]終嬰疾甚之亂，多見其不知量也。

translations.literal: Yet Jian, with a mere single basketful, wished alone to dam the rivers and seas, [3] in the end meeting the chaos of extreme illness—mostly one sees he did not know his measure.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The historian judges Zhang Jian a fool to think one man could dam the torrent of eunuch power.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]違，避也。

translations.literal: [4] Comment [1]: wei means to flee or avoid.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Note [1]: wei means to flee.

sentences.zh: 史記魏齊，魏之諸公子也。

translations.literal: The Records of the Historian: Wei Qi was a noble son of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qi was a Wei nobleman.

sentences.zh: 虞卿，趙相也。

translations.literal: Yu Qing was chancellor of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qing served as chancellor of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 范睢入秦，為昭王相，昭王乃遺趙王書曰：「魏齊，范睢之仇也，急持其頭來。

translations.literal: Fan Ju entered Qin and became chancellor to King Zhao; King Zhao then sent a letter to the king of Zhao saying, "Wei Qi is Fan Ju's enemy—hurry and bring his head."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ju demanded Wei Qi's head from Zhao.

sentences.zh: 」趙王乃圍齊，齊急亡，見虞卿。

translations.literal: " The king of Zhao thereupon besieged Qi; Qi fled in haste and saw Yu Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao besieged Wei Qi, who fled to Yu Qing.

sentences.zh: 卿度趙王不可說，乃解其印，與齊往信陵君所。

translations.literal: Qing judged the king of Zhao could not be persuaded, thereupon unfastened his seal and with Qi went to Lord Xinling.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qing resigned his seal and fled with Wei Qi to Lord Xinling.

sentences.zh: 信陵君初聞之疑，後乃出迎。

translations.literal: Lord Xinling at first hearing was doubtful, afterward came out to welcome them.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Xinling hesitated, then welcomed them.

sentences.zh: 齊聞信陵初疑，遂自刎。

translations.literal: Qi heard that Xinling had first doubted, thereupon cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qi killed himself when he heard of the hesitation.

sentences.zh: 趙王持其頭遺秦也。

translations.literal: The king of Zhao took his head and sent it to Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao sent Wei Qi's head to Qin.

sentences.zh: 注[二]季布，楚人。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Ji Bu was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Ji Bu was a Chu man.

sentences.zh: 為項羽將，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: He was a general of Xiang Yu and repeatedly embarrassed the king of Han.
translations.idiomatic: He had embarrassed Liu Bang under Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 羽敗，漢購求布千金，敢捨匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Yu was defeated, Han offered a thousand in gold for Bu; whoever dared shelter him would suffer guilt extending to three clans.
translations.idiomatic: Han put a thousand-catty price on his head.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，髡鉗布，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou family of Puyang; they shaved and collared Bu, took him to the Zhu family of Lu and sold him.
translations.idiomatic: He was disguised as a convict laborer and sold to Zhu Jia in Lu.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知是季布也。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia in his heart knew this was Ji Bu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him.

sentences.zh: 買置田舍。

translations.literal: He bought him and placed him on a farmstead.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia bought him and hid him on a farm.

sentences.zh: 乃往洛陽，見汝陰侯灌嬰，說之曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: He then went to Luoyang, saw Marquis of Ruoyin Guan Ying, and persuaded him, saying, "What crime had Ji Bu?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia went to Luoyang and persuaded Marquis of Ruoyin Guan Ying to plead for Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 臣各為主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each served their masters—it is duty.
translations.idiomatic: He said each side served its lord by duty.

sentences.zh: 」汝陰侯言於高帝，帝乃赦之。

translations.literal: " The marquis of Ruoyin spoke to the high emperor, and the emperor thereupon pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Guan Ying persuaded Gaozu to pardon Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 拜郎中，後為河東守也。

translations.literal: He was appointed gentleman of the palace, later became governor of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu rose to governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 注[三]堙，塞也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: yin means to block or dam.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: yin means to dam up.

sentences.zh: 前書班固曰：「何武、王嘉，區區以一簣障江河，用沒其身。」

translations.literal: The former book's Ban Gu says, "He Wu and Wang Jia—with a single basketful tried to dam the rivers and seas, and so lost their lives."
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu compared them to men who tried to dam a river with one basket of earth.

sentences.zh: 注[四]論語曰：「人而不仁，疾之以甚，亂也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: the Analects says, "If men are not humane and you hate them to excess, that is disorder."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: the Analects warns against excessive hatred of the inhumane.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「人雖欲自絕，其何傷於日月[乎]？

translations.literal: " It also says, "Though a man may wish to cut himself off, what does that harm sun and moon?
translations.idiomatic: The Analects compares slander to trying to eclipse the sun.

sentences.zh: 多見其不知量也。」

translations.literal: Mostly one sees he did not know his measure.
translations.idiomatic: The closing line marks folly and overreach.

sentences.zh: 岑晊字公孝，南陽棘陽人也。

translations.literal: Cen Zhi, courtesy name Gongxiao, was a man of Jiyang in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Zhi, style Gongxiao, came from Jiyang in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: [一]父*

translations.literal: His father
translations.idiomatic: His father

translations.literal: (xiang [gloss fragment])
translations.idiomatic: A damaged gloss follows the father's name.

sentences.zh: **[豫]*，為南郡太守，以貪叨誅死。

translations.literal: Yu, was governor of Nan commandery and was executed for greed.
translations.idiomatic: His father Yu governed Nan commandery and was executed for corruption.

sentences.zh: [二]晊年少未知名，往候同郡宗慈，慈方以有道見征，賓客滿門，以晊非良家子，不肯見。

translations.literal: [2] Zhi in youth was not yet known; he went to wait on Zong Ci of the same commandery; Ci was just then summoned for possession of the Way, and guests filled his gate; because Zhi was not from a good family he would not see him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Zong Ci at first snubbed the son of an executed official.

sentences.zh: 晊留門下數日，晚乃引入。

translations.literal: Zhi stayed at the gate several days; only late did he introduce him.
translations.idiomatic: After days at the gate Cen Zhi was finally admitted.

sentences.zh: 慈與語，大奇之，遂將俱至洛陽，因詣太學受業。

translations.literal: Ci spoke with him, greatly marveled, and thereupon led him together to Luoyang, whereupon he went to the Imperial Academy to take instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Zong Ci brought him to the capital and enrolled him in the academy.

sentences.zh: 注[一]棘音力。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: ji is read like li.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the commentary gives the reading of ji.

sentences.zh: 注[二]方言曰：「叨，殘也。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the Fang yan says, "Tao means remnant or cruel."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: tao in the Fang yan means cruel greed.

sentences.zh: 晊有高才，郭林宗、朱公叔等皆為友，李膺、王暢稱其有干國器，雖在閭裡，慨然有董正天下之志。

translations.literal: Zhi had high talent; Guo Tai, Zhu Gongshu, and others were his friends; Li Ying and Wang Chang praised him as having the vessel to order the state; though in the lanes and hamlets he was moved with resolve to have the ambition to oversee and correct all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai and Li Ying hailed him as a statesman even while he was still unknown locally.

sentences.zh: [一]太守弘農成□下車，欲振威嚴，聞晊高名，請為功曹，又以張牧為中賊曹吏。

translations.literal: [1] Governor Cheng [Jin] of Hongnong took office, wishing to display awesome dignity; hearing Zhi's lofty name, he asked him to be merit assessor and also appointed Zhang Mu clerk of the middle banditry bureau.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Governor Cheng Jin of Hongnong named him merit assessor alongside Zhang Mu.

sentences.zh: □委心晊、牧，□善糾違，肅清朝府。

translations.literal: Cheng entrusted his heart to Zhi and Mu; they were good at correcting violations and made court and commandery stern.
translations.idiomatic: The governor left discipline to the pair and the yamen ran clean.

sentences.zh: 宛有富賈張泛者，桓帝美人之外親，善巧雕鏤玩好之物，頗以賂遺中官，以此並得顯位，恃其伎巧，用埶縱橫。

translations.literal: In Wan there was a rich merchant Zhang Fan, a kinsman by marriage of an imperial beauty of Emperor Huan, skilled in clever carving of curio objects; he much used bribes to inner attendants and thereby together obtained prominent position; relying on his craft, he threw his weight about.
translations.idiomatic: A well-connected merchant named Zhang Fan bullied Wan with eunuch backing.

sentences.zh: 晊與牧勸□收捕泛等，既而遇赦，晊竟誅之，並收其宗族賓客，殺二百餘人，後乃奏聞。

translations.literal: Zhi and Mu urged Cheng to seize Fan and his like; afterward they met an amnesty, yet Zhi in the end executed them, together seizing their clan and retainers, killing more than two hundred persons, only afterward memorializing.
translations.idiomatic: They executed Zhang Fan and two hundred kin despite an amnesty, then reported it.

sentences.zh: 於是中常侍侯覽使泛妻上書訟其冤。

translations.literal: Thereupon Regular Attendant Hou Lan had Fan's wife submit a memorial suing for his wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Lan had the widow petition for revenge.

sentences.zh: 帝大震怒，征□，下獄死。

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly shaken with wrath, summoned Cheng, cast him into prison, and he died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had Governor Cheng jailed until he died.

sentences.zh: 晊與牧亡匿齊魯之閒。

translations.literal: Zhi and Mu fled and hid between Qi and Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Cen Zhi and Zhang Mu fled to the Qi-Lu border.

sentences.zh: 會赦出。

translations.literal: They met an amnesty and came out.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty freed them.

sentences.zh: 後州郡察舉，三府交辟，並不就。

translations.literal: Later the province and commandery examined and recommended them; the three offices jointly summoned them—none accepted.
translations.idiomatic: They refused every later summons.

sentences.zh: 及李、杜之誅，因復逃竄，終於江夏山中雲。

translations.literal: When Li and Du were executed they fled again and in the end were lost in the mountains of Jiangxia.
translations.idiomatic: After Li Ying and Du Mi died they vanished into the Jiangxia hills.

sentences.zh: 注[一]爾雅曰：「董，督正也。」

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Erya says, "Dong means to oversee and correct."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: dong in the Erya means to set right.

sentences.zh: 陳翔字子麟，汝南邵陵人也。

translations.literal: Chen Xiang, courtesy name Zilin, was a man of Shaoling in Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xiang, style Zilin, came from Shaoling in Runan.

sentences.zh: 祖父珍，司隸校尉。

translations.literal: His grandfather Zhen was metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Chen Zhen had been metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 翔少知名，善交結。

translations.literal: Xiang in youth was known for name and was good at making connections.
translations.idiomatic: He was famous young for networking.

sentences.zh: 察孝廉，太尉周景辟舉高第，拜侍御史。

translations.literal: Examined filial and incorrupt, summoned by Grand Commandant Zhou Jing and raised in high rank, appointed attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Jing raised him to attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 時正旦朝賀，大將軍梁冀威儀不整，*[翔]*奏冀恃貴不敬，請收案罪，時人奇之。

translations.literal: At the time on New Year's court congratulation Great General Liang Ji's awe-inspiring array was not orderly; Xiang memorialized that Ji relied on nobility and was disrespectful, asking to seize and charge him—people of the time marveled.
translations.idiomatic: He impeached Liang Ji for slovenly court demeanor at New Year.

sentences.zh: 遷定襄太守，征拜議郎，遷揚州刺史。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Dingxiang, summoned gentleman consultant, promoted inspector of Yangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: He governed Dingxiang, then inspected Yangzhou.

sentences.zh: 舉奏豫章太守王永奏事中官，吳郡太守徐參在職貪穢，並征詣廷尉。

translations.literal: He raised a memorial impeaching Governor of Yuzhang Wang Yong for submitting memorials through inner attendants and Governor of Wu Commandery Xu Can for corruption in office; both were summoned to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: He charged two governors—one for using eunuchs, one for graft.

sentences.zh: 參，中常侍璜之弟也。

translations.literal: Can was the younger brother of Regular Attendant Xu Huang.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Can was the eunuch Xu Huang's brother.

sentences.zh: 由此威名大振。

translations.literal: From this his awesome name greatly shook.
translations.idiomatic: His fame exploded after those indictments.

sentences.zh: 又征拜議郎，補御史中丞。

translations.literal: Again summoned gentleman consultant, supplemented imperial secretary.
translations.idiomatic: He returned as consultant and imperial secretary.

sentences.zh: 坐黨事考黃門北寺獄，以無驗見原，卒於家。

translations.literal: Sitting on the partisan affair he was examined in the Yellow Gate North Prison; because there was no proof he was released and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: The partisan case jailed him briefly; lack of evidence freed him and he died at home.

sentences.zh: 注[二]家學尚書。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the family studied the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: his household specialized in the Documents.

sentences.zh: 苑康字仲真，勃海重合人也。

translations.literal: Yuan Kang, courtesy name Zhongzhen, was a man of Chonghe in Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Kang, style Zhongzhen, came from Chonghe in Bohai.

sentences.zh: [一]少受業太學，與郭林宗親善。

translations.literal: [1] In youth he received instruction at the Imperial Academy and was intimate with Guo Tai.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He studied at the academy with Guo Tai.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，再遷穎陰令，有能多。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, twice promoted magistrate of Yingyin—he had unusual merit.
translations.idiomatic: He twice governed Yingyin with distinction.

sentences.zh: 注[一]重合，縣，故城在今滄州樂陵縣東。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Chonghe is a county; its old walled city lies east of Leling in Cangzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: old Chonghe lay east of Leling, Hebei.

sentences.zh: 遷太山太守。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: He became governor of Taishan.

sentences.zh: 郡內豪姓多不法，康至，奮威怒，施嚴令，莫有干犯者。

translations.literal: Powerful surnames within the commandery mostly acted unlawfully; when Kang arrived he roused awesome wrath and applied stern orders—none dared offend.
translations.idiomatic: He terrified Taishan's magnates into obedience.

sentences.zh: 先所請奪人田宅，皆遽還之。

translations.literal: Fields and houses they had earlier seized from people were all hurriedly returned.
translations.idiomatic: He forced restitution of stolen land.

sentences.zh: 是時山陽張儉殺常侍侯覽母，案其宗黨賓客，或有迸匿太山界者，康既常疾閹官，因此皆窮相收掩，無得遺脫。

translations.literal: At that time Zhang Jian of Shanyang had killed Regular Attendant Hou Lan's mother; investigating his clan and retainers, some had fled and hidden within Taishan borders; Kang always hated inner attendants and on this account all were hunted and seized, none escaping.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Jian's purge drove Hou Lan's clients into Taishan, Yuan Kang hunted every one of them down.

sentences.zh: 覽大怨之，誣康與兗州刺史第五種及都尉壺嘉詐上賊降，征康詣廷尉獄，減死罪一等，徙日南。

translations.literal: Lan greatly resented him and slandered Kang with Inspector of Yanzhou Di Wu and commandant Hu Jia for falsely reporting bandit surrender; Kang was summoned to the commandant of justice prison, sentence reduced one grade from death, banished to Rinan.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Lan framed him with Di Wu; he was spared death but exiled south.

sentences.zh: 穎陰人及太山羊陟等詣闕為訟，乃原還本郡，卒於家。

translations.literal: Men of Yingyin and Yang Zhi of Taishan went to the palace to argue his case; thereupon he was pardoned to return to his native commandery and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Popular petition won his return home, where he died.

sentences.zh: □□字文有，山陽瑕丘人也。

translations.literal: [Name damaged in text], courtesy name Wenyou, was a man of Xiaqiu in Shanyang.
translations.idiomatic: A scholar whose name is lost in the lacuna, style Wenyou, came from Xiaqiu in Shanyang.

sentences.zh: [一]少為諸生，家貧而志清，不受鄉里施惠。

translations.literal: [1] In youth he was a student; his family was poor but his will was pure; he would not accept favors from his village.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He was a poor student who refused charity.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，連辟公府，皆不就。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, repeatedly summoned to ducal offices—none did he accept.
translations.idiomatic: He turned down every high summons.

sentences.zh: 立精舍教授，遠方至者常數百人。

translations.literal: He established a refined hall for teaching; those who came from afar were always several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: He ran a private school of several hundred students.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時，博士征，不就。

translations.literal: In Emperor Huan's time he was summoned as erudite; he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: He refused an erudite post under Emperor Huan.

sentences.zh: 靈帝即位，太尉黃瓊舉方正，對策合時宜，再遷議郎，補蒙令。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ling took the throne, Grand Commandant Huang Qiong recommended him upright and correct; his palace answers matched the times; twice promoted gentleman consultant, supplemented magistrate of Meng.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong nominated him; he served as magistrate of Meng.

sentences.zh: [二]以郡守非其人，□官去。

translations.literal: [2] Because the governor was not the right man he left office.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He quit over a bad governor.

sentences.zh: 家無產業，子孫同衣而出。

translations.literal: His household had no property; sons and grandsons went out in the same clothes.
translations.idiomatic: His family shared one set of good clothes for going out.

sentences.zh: 年八十，卒於家。

translations.literal: At age eighty he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at eighty.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]瑕丘，今兗州縣。

translations.literal: [3] Comment [1]: Xiaqiu is a county in Yanzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note [1]: Xiaqiu is in modern Shandong.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蒙，縣，屬梁國。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Meng is a county belonging to the kingdom of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Meng county lay in the kingdom of Liang.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰「□*[與]*子孫同衣而行，并日而食」也。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says, "[He] went out with sons and grandsons in the same clothes and ate only every other day."
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng stresses his extreme poverty.

sentences.zh: 劉儒字叔林，東郡陽平人也。

translations.literal: Liu Ru, courtesy name Shulin, was a man of Yangping in Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ru, style Shulin, came from Yangping in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]郭林宗常謂儒口訥心辯，有珪璋之質。

translations.literal: [1] Guo Tai often said Ru was slow of tongue but keen in mind, with the substance of jade tablets.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Guo Tai called him tongue-tied but brilliant as jade.

sentences.zh: [二] 察孝廉，舉高第，三遷侍中。

translations.literal: [2] Examined filial and incorrupt, raised in high rank, thrice promoted palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He rose thrice to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時，數有□異，下策博求直言，儒上封事十條，極言得失，辭甚忠切。

translations.literal: In Emperor Huan's time there were repeated prodigies; an edict broadly sought outspoken counsel; Ru submitted a sealed document in ten articles, speaking to the utmost of gain and loss; the wording was very loyal and cutting.
translations.idiomatic: He sent ten blunt memorials on omens and policy.

sentences.zh: 帝不能納，出為任城相。

translations.literal: The emperor could not adopt them and sent him out as chancellor of Rencheng.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ignored him and made him chancellor of Rencheng.

sentences.zh: 頃之，征拜議郎。

translations.literal: Before long he was summoned gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Soon he was recalled as consultant.

sentences.zh: 會竇武事，下獄自殺。

translations.literal: Meeting the affair of Dou Wu he was cast into prison and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Wu's fall drove him to suicide in prison.

sentences.zh: 注[一]陽平故城，今魏州莘縣。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Yangping's old walled city is in Shen county in Weizhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: old Yangping is near modern Shen, Henan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]珪璋，玉也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: gui and zhang are jade; half a gui is called zhang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: gui and zhang are ritual jades.

sentences.zh: 半珪曰璋。

translations.literal: Half a gui is called zhang.
translations.idiomatic: A zhang is half a scepter jade.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰：「林宗歎儒有珪璋之質，終必為令德之士。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says, "Linzong sighed that Ru had the substance of gui and zhang and in the end would be a man of fine virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai predicted Liu Ru would be a man of virtue.

sentences.zh: 」詩曰：「如珪如璋，令聞令望。」

translations.literal: " The Odes says, "Like gui, like zhang—fine fame, fine expectation.
translations.idiomatic: The ode compares a gentleman to scepter jades.

sentences.zh: 賈彪字偉節，穎川定陵人也。

translations.literal: Jia Biao, courtesy name Weijie, was a man of Dingling in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Biao, style Weijie, came from Dingling in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 少游京師，志節慷慨，與同郡荀爽齊名。

translations.literal: In youth he traveled the capital; his purpose and integrity were impassioned; with Xun Shuang of the same commandery he shared equal fame.
translations.idiomatic: In Luoyang he matched Xun Shuang for fervor and fame.

sentences.zh: 初仕州郡，舉孝廉，補新息長。

translations.literal: At first he served province and commandery, was recommended filial and incorrupt, supplemented as chief of Xinxi.
translations.idiomatic: He began as chief of Xinxi.

sentences.zh: [一]小民困貧，多不養子，彪嚴為其制，與殺人同罪。

translations.literal: [1] The little people were distressed in poverty and mostly did not rear sons; Biao strictly set regulations for it, making it the same crime as murder.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He criminalized infanticide in his county.

sentences.zh: 城南有盜劫害人者，北有婦人殺子者，彪出案發，[二]而掾吏欲引南。

translations.literal: South of the city were bandits who robbed and killed people; north of the city was a woman who killed her child; Biao went out to investigate the cases, [2] but the clerks wished to lead him south.
translations.idiomatic: [2] His clerks wanted him to pursue the bandits first.

sentences.zh: 彪怒曰：「賊寇害人，此則常理，母子相殘，逆天違道。

translations.literal: Biao angrily said, "Bandits who harm people are the usual pattern; mother and child destroying each other defies heaven and violates the Way."
translations.idiomatic: He said parricide was worse than robbery.

sentences.zh: 」遂驅車北行，案驗其罪。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he drove the carriage north, examined and verified her crime.
translations.idiomatic: He drove north to try the mother first.

sentences.zh: 城南賊聞之，亦面縛自首。

translations.literal: The southern bandits heard it and also bound themselves and surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: The robbers surrendered when they heard of his justice.

sentences.zh: 數年閒，人養子者千數，僉曰「賈父所長」，生男名為「賈子」，生女名為「賈女」。

translations.literal: Within several years people who reared sons numbered in the thousands; all said, "What Chief Jia lengthens," naming sons "Jia's sons" and daughters "Jia's daughters."
translations.idiomatic: Within a few years a thousand families kept newborns and nicknamed them after Magistrate Jia.

sentences.zh: 注[一]新息，今豫州縣。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Xinxi is a county in Yuzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xinxi is in modern Henan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]就發處案驗之。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: investigate at the place where the affair broke out.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: the gloss means to verify on site.

sentences.zh: 延熹九年，黨事起，太尉陳蕃爭之不能得，朝廷寒心，莫敢復言。

translations.literal: In the ninth year of Yanxi the partisan affair arose; Grand Commandant Chen Fan argued but could not prevail; the court went cold at heart and none dared speak again.
translations.idiomatic: In 166 the proscription silenced the whole court after Chen Fan failed.

sentences.zh: 彪謂同志曰：

translations.literal: Biao said to his comrades,
translations.idiomatic: Jia Biao told his allies,

sentences.zh: 「吾不西行，大禍不解。

translations.literal: "If I do not go west, the great disaster will not be resolved."
translations.idiomatic: that he must go to Luoyang or the purge would stand.

sentences.zh: 」乃入洛陽，說城門校尉竇武、尚書霍諝，武等訟之，桓帝以此大赦黨人。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he entered Luoyang, persuaded Colonel of the City Gates Dou Wu and Minister Secretary Huo Xu; Wu and the others pleaded the case, and Emperor Huan on this account greatly pardoned the partisans.
translations.idiomatic: He lobbied Dou Wu and Huo Xu until Emperor Huan issued a broad amnesty.

sentences.zh: 李膺出，曰：「吾得免此，賈生之謀也。」

translations.literal: Li Ying came out and said, "That I could escape this is the plan of Jia Sheng."
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying credited Jia Biao with their release.

sentences.zh: 先是岑晊以黨事逃亡，親友多匿焉，彪獨閉門不納，時人望之。

translations.literal: Earlier Cen Zhi had fled on the partisan affair; many relatives and friends hid him, but Biao alone barred his gate and would not admit him—people of the time resented him.
translations.idiomatic: When Cen Zhi fled, Jia Biao alone refused him shelter.

sentences.zh: [一]彪曰：「傳言『相時而動，無累後人』。

translations.literal: [1] Biao said, "The tradition says, Move when the season fits—do not burden those who come after."
translations.idiomatic: [1] He quoted the need to act in season and not burden posterity.

sentences.zh: [二]公孝以要君致釁，自遺其咎，吾以不能奮戈相待，反可容隱之乎？

translations.literal: [2] Gongxiao by importuning his ruler brought disaster and invited blame himself—should I, instead of raising a spear to meet him, on the contrary harbor him in concealment?
translations.idiomatic: [2] He said Cen Zhi had courted ruin and hiding him would wrong the state.

sentences.zh: 」於是鹹服其裁正。

translations.literal: " Thereupon all submitted to his judgment.
translations.idiomatic: Critics accepted his reasoning.

sentences.zh: 注[一]望，怨也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: wang means resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: wang here means blame or resentment.

sentences.zh: 注[二]相，視也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: xiang means to observe.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: xiang means to watch the moment.

sentences.zh: 左傳之文也。

translations.literal: It is text from the Zuo Commentary.
translations.idiomatic: The quotation comes from the Zuo Commentary.

sentences.zh: 以黨禁錮，卒於家。

translations.literal: Because of partisan proscription he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: The ban kept him home until he died.

sentences.zh: 初，彪兄弟三人，並有高名，而彪最優，故天下稱曰「賈氏三虎，偉節最怒」。

translations.literal: At first the Biao brothers were three, all with lofty name, but Biao was the finest; therefore the realm called them "the three Jia tigers—Weijie is the fiercest."
translations.idiomatic: The world ranked the three Jia brothers as tigers, with Jia Biao fiercest.

sentences.zh: 何顒字伯求，南陽襄鄉人也。

translations.literal: He Yong, courtesy name Boqiu, was a man of Xiangxiang in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: He Yong, style Boqiu, came from Xiangxiang in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: [一]少遊學洛陽。

translations.literal: [1] In youth he studied abroad in Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He studied in the capital as a young man.

sentences.zh: 顒雖後進，而郭林宗、賈偉節等與之相好，顯名太學。

translations.literal: Though Yong came later, Guo Tai, Jia Biao, and others befriended him, and he became famous at the Imperial Academy.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai befriended him despite his junior status and made him famous at the academy.

sentences.zh: 友人虞偉高有父讎未報，而篤病將終，顒往候之，偉高泣而訴。

translations.literal: His friend Yu Weigao had a father's enemy unavenged while gravely ill and near death; Yong went to visit him; Weigao wept and poured out his plea.
translations.idiomatic: He visited a dying friend who wept over an unavenged father.

sentences.zh: 顒感其義，為復讎，以頭醊其墓。

translations.literal: Yong was moved by his righteousness, avenged him for him, and with the head poured libation at his tomb.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged the murder and poured a blood libation on the friend's grave.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]襄鄉故城在今隨州棗陽縣東北也。

translations.literal: [2] Comment [1]: Xiangxiang's old walled city lies northeast of Zaoyang in Suizhou today.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Note [1]: old Xiangxiang lay northeast of modern Zaoyang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]醊，祭酹也，音竹歲反。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: zhui means to pour a libation; the reading is the zhu-sui fan cut.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: zhui means a libation offering; the commentary gives fanqie.

sentences.zh: 及陳蕃、李膺之敗，顒以與蕃、膺善，遂為宦官所陷，乃變姓名，亡匿汝南閒。

translations.literal: When Chen Fan and Li Ying fell, Yong, because he was on good terms with Fan and Ying, was then trapped by eunuchs; he changed his name and fled to hide in the spaces of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: After Chen Fan and Li Ying fell, he fled under an alias in Runan.

sentences.zh: 所至皆親其豪桀，有聲荊豫之域。

translations.literal: Wherever he went he drew close to its bold spirits and had reputation in the Jing and Yu regions.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied heroes wherever he went across Jing and Yu.

sentences.zh: 袁紹慕之，私與往來，結為奔走之友。

translations.literal: Yuan Shao admired him, privately associated with him, and bonded as running-service friends.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao courted him as a confidential ally.

sentences.zh: [一] 是時黨事起，天下多離其難，顒常私入洛陽，從紹計議。

translations.literal: [1] At this time the partisan affair arose; many under heaven suffered its hardship; Yong often secretly entered Luoyang to consult with Shao.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He slipped into Luoyang repeatedly to plot with Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 其窮困閉□者，為求援救，以濟其患。

translations.literal: Those who were destitute and blocked [lacuna] he sought aid and rescue for, to relieve their distress.
translations.idiomatic: He organized relief for partisans trapped in poverty.

sentences.zh: 有被掩捕者，則廣設權計，使得逃隱，全免者甚觿。

translations.literal: Those who were seized in surprise arrests he broadly set ruses for, enabling them to flee and hide; those who wholly escaped were very many.
translations.idiomatic: He engineered escapes for many men under warrant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩大雅曰：「予曰有胥附，予曰有先後，予曰有奔走，予曰有禦侮。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Da ya of the Odes says, "I said I have attendants, I said I have forerunners, I said I have runners, I said I have defenders."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the ode lists the king's four kinds of helper.

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注曰：「諭德宣譽曰奔走。」

translations.literal: " Mao Chang's commentary says, "To proclaim virtue and spread fame is called running service.
translations.idiomatic: Mao Hong defines the runner as one who spreads the ruler's fame.

sentences.zh: 操以是嘉之。

translations.literal: Cao on this account praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Cao honored him for it.

sentences.zh: 嘗稱「穎川荀彧，王佐之器」。

translations.literal: He once declared, "Xun Yu of Yingchuan has the vessel of a king's assistant."
translations.idiomatic: He famously called Xun Yu fit to assist a true king.

sentences.zh: 及彧為尚書令，遣人西迎叔父爽，並致顒屍，而葬之爽之頤傍。

translations.literal: When Yu became minister secretary, he sent men west to welcome his uncle Shuang and also brought Yong's corpse and buried it beside Shuang's tomb mound.
translations.idiomatic: When Xun Yu rose to power he had He Yong buried beside Xun Shuang.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：渭以涇濁，玉以礫貞。

translations.literal: The encomium says: The Wei is clear because the Jing is muddy; jade shows chaste because of gravel.
translations.idiomatic: The encomium compares worthies clarified by contrast with vileness.

sentences.zh: 物性既區，嗜惡從形。

translations.literal: Things' natures are already divided; likes and dislikes follow form.
translations.idiomatic: Nature sorts noble from base; preference follows what one sees.

sentences.zh: [一]蘭蕕無並，銷長相傾。

translations.literal: [1] Orchid and stinkgrass do not grow together; waning and waxing overturn each other.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Sweet orchid cannot share soil with stinkgrass; the two camps exclude each other.

sentences.zh: [二]徒恨芳膏，煎灼燈明。

translations.literal: [2] In vain we regret fragrant oil that burns the bright lamp.
translations.idiomatic: [2] It laments fine men consumed like lamp oil by the flame of integrity.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]礫音歷。

translations.literal: [3] Comment [1]: li is read like li for gravel.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note [1]: fanqie for the gravel character.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「礫，小石也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says, "Li means small stone.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines li as pebble.

sentences.zh: 」言渭以涇濁，乃顯其清，玉居礫石，乃見其貞。

translations.literal: " It means the Wei is muddy with the Jing and thereby shows its clarity; jade lies among gravel and thereby shows its chasteness.
translations.idiomatic: Mud clarifies the river; gravel sets off the jade.

sentences.zh: 區猶別也。

translations.literal: Qu means distinction.
translations.idiomatic: Qu means sorting into kinds.

sentences.zh: 嗜，愛也。

translations.literal: Shi means love.
translations.idiomatic: Shi means fondness.

sentences.zh: 從形謂形有善惡也。

translations.literal: Cong xing means forms have good and evil.
translations.idiomatic: Cong xing means outward form shows good or evil.

sentences.zh: 以諭彼李膺等與宦豎不同，故相憎疾。

translations.literal: It likens how Li Ying and the others differed from the eunuchs, therefore mutually hated.
translations.idiomatic: It applies the image to Li Ying's hatred of the eunuchs.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蕕，臭草也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: you is a stinking grass.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: you is the foul elecampane-like weed.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「一熏一蕕，十年尚猶有華。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says, "One incense, one stink—after ten years there is still odor."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo contrasts one fragrant and one foul influence.

sentences.zh: 」易否卦曰：「小人道長，君子道銷。

translations.literal: " The hexagram Pi says, "The petty man's way waxes, the gentleman's way wanes.
translations.idiomatic: The Pi hexagram shows the petty man rising.

sentences.zh: 」泰卦曰：「君子道長，小人道銷。

translations.literal: " The hexagram Tai says, "The gentleman's way waxes, the petty man's way wanes.
translations.idiomatic: The Tai hexagram reverses the pattern.

sentences.zh: 」老子曰「高下相傾」也。

translations.literal: " Laozi says, "High and low overturn each other.
translations.idiomatic: Laozi notes that high and low define each other.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書龔勝死，有一老父入哭甚哀，曰：「熏以香自燒，膏以明自銷。」

translations.literal: Comment [3]: when Gong Sheng of the former book died, an old man entered weeping bitterly and said, "Incense burns itself with fragrance; oil consumes itself with brightness."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: an old man mourned Gong Sheng with the lamp-and-incense metaphor.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Section heading: textual collation notes.

sentences.zh: 二一八四頁六行又將及難按：「又」原斗「及」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2184 line 6: again will meet disaster—note: the original misprinted you as ji; corrected per Ji edition and Palace edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: emendation from you to ji at 2184.6.

sentences.zh: 二一八四頁一一行狙獮猴也按：「獮」原斗「彌」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2184 line 11: ju macaque—note: the original misprinted yi for xian; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restore xian for yi in the macaque gloss.

sentences.zh: 二一八四頁一三行謂范睢蔡澤之類按：汲本、殿本「睢」作「雎」。

translations.literal: Page 2184 line 13: meaning Fan Ju and Cai Ze types—note: Ji and Palace editions write Ju with the other sui graph.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: variant spelling of Fan Ju's name.

sentences.zh: 二一八四頁一四行贏糧而景從也按：「贏」原斗「嬴」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2184 line 14: carrying grain and following like a shadow—note: the original misprinted ying for ying; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: fix ying carrying grain.

sentences.zh: 二一八四頁一六行懷經協術集解引惠棟說，謂「協」當作「挾」，古字通，黃瓊傳「黃門協邪」是也。

translations.literal: Page 2184 line 16: clutching the classics and carrying technique—Hui Dong in the collected commentary argues xie should read xie carry, an ancient interchange, as in Huang Qiong's memorial on eunuch wickedness.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: xie for xie per Hui Dong.

sentences.zh: 二一八五頁二行忠義之流按：「忠」原斗「志」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2185 line 2: the class of loyal righteousness—note: the original misprinted zhi for zhong; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: zhong not zhi.

sentences.zh: 二一八五頁三行國命委於閹寺按：「閹」原斗「閽」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2185 line 3: the state's mandate entrusted to eunuch temples—note: the original misprinted hun for yan; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: yan for hun.

sentences.zh: 二一八五頁一五行正枉必過其直見孟子按：殿本考證謂今孟子無此文。

translations.literal: Page 2185 line 15: straightening the crooked must pass its straight—see Mencius—note: the Palace edition investigation says this sentence is not in the present Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Mencius parallel not found in received text.

sentences.zh: 二一八六頁一０行磬*

translations.literal: Page 2186 line 10: qing*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: damaged graph at 2186.10.

translations.literal: (ya)
translations.idiomatic: Collation gloss fragment ya.

sentences.zh: **[互]*境界按：校補引柳從辰說，謂「牙」應作「□」，即「互」字。

translations.literal: **[mutual]* borderlands—note: collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: ya should read hu, i.e. the graph mutual.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: read hu for ya in borderlands phrase.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: emendation adopted.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁二行李膺為河南尹集解引惠棟說，謂考異云「河南尹」當作「司隸」。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 2: Li Ying as governor of Henan—collected commentary cites Hui Dong: textual investigation says governor of Henan should read metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Li Ying's title Henan versus metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 校補引侯康說，謂通鑒系張成事於延熹九年，是年李膺為司隸，故考異云然，然靈紀九年無赦，惟八年三月大赦天下，則張成推占當赦，命子殺人，實在八年三月前，是時李膺正代鄧萬世為河南尹也。

translations.literal: Collation supplement cites Hou Kang: the Comprehensive Mirror dates Zhang Cheng's affair in the ninth year of Yanxi, when Li Ying was metropolitan commandant, hence the investigation; yet Ling's annals have no amnesty in the ninth year, only a great amnesty in the third month of the eighth year, so Zhang Cheng's divination of amnesty and order to kill must fall before the third month of the eighth year, when Li Ying had just replaced Deng Wanshi as governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: dating Li Ying's title during Zhang Cheng case.

sentences.zh: 今按：黃山謂按張成事不必在八年。

translations.literal: Now note: Huang Shan argues the Zhang Cheng affair need not fall in the eighth year.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Huang Shan on dating.

sentences.zh: 膺之輸作左校，本傳及陳蕃傳皆謂膺河南尹，馮緄傳則謂膺司隸校尉，此范書之疏繆也。

translations.literal: Ying's labor at Left Work—this biography and Chen Fan's both say Ying was governor of Henan; Feng Gun's says metropolitan commandant—this is Fan Ye's inconsistency.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Fan Ye's inconsistency on Li Ying's office.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁三行帝亦頗誶其占集解引錢大昕說，謂「誶」當作「訊」，古書訊誶二字多相亂。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 3: the emperor also somewhat ridiculed his divination—collected commentary cites Qian Daxin: sui should read xun; in old books xun and sui often interchange.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: read xun for sui.

sentences.zh: 今按：御覽六五一引作「訊」。

translations.literal: Now note: Imperial Readings 651 quotes xun.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Taiping yulan supports xun.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁三行成弟子牢修集解引惠棟說，謂袁宏紀作「牢順」，續漢志作「牢川」。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 3: Zhang Cheng's disciple Lao Xiu—collected commentary cites Hui Dong: Yuan Hong's annals write Lao Shun; continuation Han treatise writes Lao Chuan.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: variants for Lao Xiu's name.

sentences.zh: 今按：御覽引作「牢循」。

translations.literal: Now note: Imperial Readings quotes Lao Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: another variant Lao Xun.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁一一行荀翌按：汲本、殿本「翌」作「昱」。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 11: Xu Yi—note: Ji and Palace editions write the graph as Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yi versus Yu for Xun Yi.

sentences.zh: 下同。

translations.literal: Same below.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: same emendation below.

sentences.zh: 按：「翌」字經史多假為「昱」字。

translations.literal: Note: in classics and histories yi is often borrowed for yu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: yi as loan for yu.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁一三行孔昱按：皇甫規傳「昱」作「翊」。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 13: Kong Yu—note: Huangfu Gui's biography writes yi as yi.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kong Yu versus Kong Yi.

sentences.zh: 集解引惠棟說，謂黨錮傳有孔昱，昱字符世，韓敕碑有御史孔翊元世，則翊即昱也。

translations.literal: Collected commentary cites Hui Dong: the partisan prohibition biography has Kong Yu, Yu courtesy name Yuan shi; the Han Jue stele has censor Kong Yi Yuan shi—therefore yi is Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kong Yi identified with Kong Yu.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁一三行苑康汲本、殿本「苑」作「范」。

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 13: Yuan Kang—Ji and Palace editions write Fan for Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Fan versus Yuan for Yuan Kang.

sentences.zh: 下同。

translations.literal: Same below.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: same below.

sentences.zh: 按：荀淑、竇武傳並作「苑康」，作「范」誤。

translations.literal: Note: Xun Shu and Dou Wu biographies both write Yuan Kang—Fan is an error.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yuan is correct, not Fan.

sentences.zh: 二一八七頁一三行□*

translations.literal: Page 2187 line 13: graph*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: lacuna at 2187.13.

translations.literal: (fu)
translations.idiomatic: Collation gloss fu.

sentences.zh: **[□]*按：集解引惠棟說，謂本傳及韓敕碑皆作「□」。

translations.literal: **[lacuna]*—note: collected commentary cites Hui Dong: biography and Han Jue stele both write the graph.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restore lacunose name per stele.

sentences.zh: 今據改，與下文合。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly to match the text below.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: emended for consistency.

sentences.zh: 二一八八頁六行劉祗按：「祗」原斗「只」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2188 line 6: Liu Zhi—note: the original misprinted zhi as zhi; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Liu Zhi graph.

sentences.zh: 二一八八頁六行朱楷按：「楷」原斗「揩」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2188 line 6: Zhu Kai—note: the original misprinted kai as kai; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Zhu Kai graph.

sentences.zh: 二一九０頁一行荀翌附祖淑傳按：沉家本謂案淑傳雲兄淑子昱，則「祖」字鬥。

translations.literal: Page 2190 line 1: Xu Yi attached to grandfather Shu's biography—note: Shen Jiaben argues the graph zu is wrong for Xun Shu's son Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: zu error in Xun Yi attachment note.

sentences.zh: 二一九０頁二行王璋字伯儀集解引惠棟說，謂「璋」當作「章」，「儀」當作「義」。

translations.literal: Page 2190 line 2: Wang Zhang courtesy Boyi—collected commentary cites Hui Dong: zhang should read zhang, yi should read yi.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Wang Zhang name variants.

sentences.zh: 按：校補引柳從辰說，謂上文王章為八廚，字本作「章」，此又作「璋」，必有一誤。

translations.literal: Note: collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: above Wang Zhang is among the Eight Kitchens, the original writes zhang—one of the two must be wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Wang Zhang versus Wang Zhang duplication.

sentences.zh: 二一九一頁七行還居綸氏續志「綸氏」作「輪氏」。

translations.literal: Page 2191 line 7: returned to dwell at Lunshi—continuation treatise writes Lunshi as Wheel clan.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Lun versus Wheel county name.

sentences.zh: 按：綸輪通。

translations.literal: Note: Lun and wheel interchange.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: lun and wheel are cognate.

sentences.zh: 二一九一頁八行為節*[志]*者所羞據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2191 line 8: shamed by men of integrity—note: supplied per Ji and Palace editions from zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supplement zhi in phrase.

sentences.zh: 二一九二頁一五行使*[使]*者往觀楚之寶器據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2192 line 15: envoy* envoy went to observe Chu's treasures—supplied per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: duplicate envoy supplied.

sentences.zh: 二一九三頁九行使守高堂按：汲本、殿本「堂」作「唐」。

translations.literal: Page 2193 line 9: ordered to guard Gaotang—note: Ji and Palace editions write Tang for hall.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Gaotang versus Gaotang.

sentences.zh: 二一九三頁一四行*[則]*弒君父矣據汲本、殿本補，與左傳合。

translations.literal: Page 2193 line 14: then murdered lord and father—supplied ze per Ji and Palace editions to match Zuo Commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supply ze in Zuo quotation.

sentences.zh: 二一九四頁七行時張讓弟朔為野王令按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀作「陽翟令張輿」，又膺為河南尹時考殺之也。

translations.literal: Page 2194 line 7: at the time Zhang Rang's younger brother Shuo was magistrate of Yewang—note: collected commentary cites Hui Dong: Yuan Hong's annals make him Zhang Yu, magistrate of Yangzhai, and Li Ying as governor of Henan when he was examined and killed.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: variant on Zhang Shuo and magistracy.

sentences.zh: 二一九四頁一一行今臣到官已積一旬按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀「一旬」作「二旬」。

translations.literal: Page 2194 line 11: now your servant has reached office already piled ten days—note: collected commentary cites Hui Dong: Yuan Hong's annals read twenty days.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: ten versus twenty days in Fan Pang memorial.

sentences.zh: 二一九四頁一三行皆鞠躬屏氣按：「鞠」原斗「鞫」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2194 line 13: all bowed the body and held breath—note: the original misprinted ju for ju; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: bow graph ju versus ju.

sentences.zh: 二一九七頁八行漏奪名籍刊誤謂「奪」當作「脫」。

translations.literal: Page 2197 line 8: leaked stripping the name register—Kan wu argues duo should read tuo slip away.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: duo versus tuo in Jing Yi quotation.

sentences.zh: 按：惠棟謂續漢書作「漏脫」，奪與脫古字通。

translations.literal: Note: Hui Dong says the Xu Han shu reads "leak and slip away"; duo and tuo were interchangeable graphs in antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong cites Xu Han shu wording and the duo-tuo interchange.

sentences.zh: 二一九八頁四行劉季陵清高士按：汲本「陵」作「林」。

translations.literal: Page 2198 line 4: Liu Jiling the lofty gentleman—note: the Ji edition writes ling as lin (forest).
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji edition has lin instead of ling for Liu Jiling.

sentences.zh: 殿本考證謂「陵」本或作「林」。

translations.literal: The Palace edition investigation says ling was sometimes written lin in older copies.
translations.idiomatic: Palace editors note the ling-lin variant.

sentences.zh: 二二０二頁六行入林慮山中按：御覽八一七引謝承書，作「遁多黑山」。

translations.literal: Page 2202 line 6: entering the Linyu mountains—note: Imperial Readings 817 quoting Xie Cheng writes "flee to Black Mountain."
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Taiping yulan variant for the mountain name.

sentences.zh: 二二０二頁七行為冶家傭按：「冶」原斗「治」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2202 line 7: hired to a foundry family—note: the original misprinted zhi for ye; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restore ye foundry graph.

sentences.zh: 二二０二頁七行追之於涅陽巿中按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀作「滏陽」，魏郡鄴縣有釜水，或是滏水之陽。

translations.literal: Page 2202 line 7: pursuing him in the Nieyang market—note: collected commentary cites Hui Dong: Yuan Hong's annals write Fuyang; Ye county in Wei commandery has the Fu kettle stream—perhaps north of the Fu water.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Nieyang versus Fuyang market name.

sentences.zh: 案漢末林慮、鄴縣皆屬魏郡，馥入林慮山，靜追之滏陽巿中，為得其實。

translations.literal: Case note: late Han Linyu and Ye county both belonged to Wei commandery; Fu entering Linyu mountain and Jing pursuing him in the Fuyang market fits the facts.
translations.idiomatic: Collation argues Fuyang fits geography better than Nieyang.

sentences.zh: 二二０四頁三行得無自取不優之議也按：汲本「議也」作「譏邪」。

translations.literal: Page 2204 line 3: could it not invite criticism of being less than magnanimous—note: Ji edition reads yi ye as ji xie.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: yi ye versus ji xie wording.
